# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Energy Management System (EnMS). List any two key benefits of implementing ISO 50001 in a food processing plant.**

**Model Answer (8–10 lines, 3 marks):**

An **Energy Management System (EnMS)** is a structured framework of policies, procedures, processes, and resources that enables an organization to systematically manage and reduce its energy consumption. It is governed by the international standard **ISO 50001:2018**, which follows the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle.

Two key benefits in a food processing plant:

1. **Sustained Energy Savings** (typically 10%–30% in 2–3 years) by identifying and controlling Significant Energy Uses (SEUs) such as boilers, evaporators, chillers, and compressed air systems.
2. **Improved Regulatory Compliance and Brand Eligibility** — ISO 50001 certification is increasingly required by multinational food buyers (e.g., Nestlé, Unilever) and helps meet PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) obligations under the Energy Conservation Act 2001 of India.
3. *Optional third point:* Integration with ISO 9001 and ISO 14001 into a single Integrated Management System (IMS), reducing audit costs.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Energy Baseline (EnB) and Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI) with one example each from a food industry context.**

**Model Answer (8–10 lines, 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Energy Baseline (EnB) | Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Quantitative reference providing a basis for comparison of energy performance over a defined historical period. | Measurable metric that expresses energy consumption normalized by a relevant production variable. |
| Nature | Static (fixed reference, valid until re-baselined) | Dynamic (changes with each reporting period) |
| Example (Bakery) | 18,000 kWh consumed in FY 2022–23 to produce 600 tonnes of bread | 30 kWh per tonne of bread baked |
| Example (Dairy) | 12,500 GJ in 2022 to process 25,000 kL milk | 0.5 GJ/kL of milk processed |

> EnB and EnPI together allow *apples-to-apples* comparison of energy performance across time, products, and even plants.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA) cycle** as applied to the implementation of **ISO 50001:2018 EnMS** in a food industry plant. List at least four key deliverables of each phase.

**(b) [7 marks]** A dairy plant recorded the following data. Calculate the **Energy Baseline**, **current EnPI**, **normalized energy consumption**, and the **percentage energy savings**.

| Parameter | Baseline Year 2021 | Current Year 2023 |
|---|---|---|
| Total Energy (GJ) | 25,000 | 22,000 |
| Production (kL milk) | 5,000 | 6,000 |
| Operating hours | 6,000 | 6,300 |

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — PDCA Cycle for EnMS [7 marks]**

**Sketch (3 marks):**

```
       ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
       │   P ──▶ D ──▶ C ──▶ A ──▶ (loop)    │
       │   ▲                          │      │
       │   └──────────────────────────┘      │
       └─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Explanation (4 marks):**

| Phase | ISO 50001 Clause | Key Deliverables |
|---|---|---|
| **PLAN** | Clauses 4–7.1 | (i) Energy Policy (ii) Energy Review (iii) EnB & EnPI (iv) Energy Objectives, Targets, Action Plans |
| **DO** | Clause 8 | (i) Competence & Awareness (ii) Communication (iii) Documented Information (iv) Operational Controls and ECM Implementation |
| **CHECK** | Clause 9 | (i) Monitoring & Measurement of EnPIs (ii) Internal Audit (iii) Legal Compliance Evaluation (iv) Top Management Review |
| **ACT** | Clause 10 | (i) Non-conformity & Corrective Action (ii) Continual Improvement (iii) Re-baselining Evaluation (iv) Update of Action Plans |

> The **PDCA loop** ensures that energy management is *iterative* and *self-correcting*, addressing the natural drift that occurs after a one-time energy audit.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Energy Baseline (EnPI_base)**

$$
\text{EnPI}_{\text{base}} = \frac{25{,}000 \; \text{GJ}}{5{,}000 \; \text{kL}} = 5.0 \; \text{GJ/kL}
$$

**Step 2: Current EnPI**

$$
\text{EnPI}_{\text{current}} = \frac{22{,}000 \; \text{GJ}}{6{,}000 \; \text{kL}} = 3.667 \; \text{GJ/kL}
$$

**Step 3: Normalized Energy Consumption (current year)**

$$
E_{\text{norm}} = 5.0 \; \frac{\text{GJ}}{\text{kL}} \times 6{,}000 \; \text{kL} = 30{,}000 \; \text{GJ}
$$

**Step 4: Absolute Energy Savings**

$$
\Delta E = 30{,}000 - 22{,}000 = 8{,}000 \; \text{GJ}
$$

**Step 5: Percentage Energy Savings**

$$
\% \Delta E = \frac{8{,}000}{30{,}000} \times 100 = 26.67\%
$$

**Step 6: Re-baselining Decision**

- Production change: $\frac{6{,}000 - 5{,}000}{5{,}000} \times 100 = +20\%$ → at the threshold.
- Operating hours change: $\frac{6{,}300 - 6{,}000}{6{,}000} \times 100 = +5\%$ → below threshold.

> *Comment:* The plant is approaching the 20% production threshold; it is **recommended** to re-baseline in 2023 to maintain the integrity of the EnMS, even if not yet mandatory.

**Conclusion:** The dairy plant has achieved a **26.67% energy intensity reduction** while increasing throughput by 20% — an exemplary EnMS outcome.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **Measurement and Verification (M&V) of energy savings** as per **ISO 50015** and **IPMVP**. Describe any two M&V options with examples from a food plant.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 22 kW induction motor driving a mixer in a bakery operates 4,800 hours/year at full load. The motor is replaced with an **IE4 (Super Premium) energy-efficient motor** with an efficiency of 93.5%, replacing the existing motor with an efficiency of 88%. Calculate the **annual energy savings** in kWh and the **monetary savings** at ₹8.00/kWh. Also compute the **simple payback period** if the new motor costs ₹95,000.

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — M&V Concepts [7 marks]**

**Definition (2 marks):** **Measurement & Verification (M&V)** is the process of quantifying and documenting the energy savings achieved by an Energy Conservation Measure (ECM), ensuring that claimed savings are real, persistent, and attributable to the implemented measure. **ISO 50015:2014** provides the ISO framework; **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement & Verification Protocol)** is the most widely used complementary protocol.

**Four M&V Options (4 marks):**

| Option | Description | Typical Application in Food Plant |
|---|---|---|
| **Option A — Retrofit Isolation: Key Parameter Measurement** | Savings calculated from a key parameter (e.g., hours of operation, % load) with short-term or spot measurements. | VFD on a pump, LED retrofit, boiler burner upgrade. |
| **Option B — Retrofit Isolation: All Parameter Measurement** | All relevant parameters measured continuously. | Chiller plant upgrade with full instrumentation. |
| **Option C — Whole Facility** | M&V applied at the utility meter level for the entire plant. | Plant-wide compressed air leak reduction program. |
| **Option D — Calibrated Simulation** | Energy model calibrated with actual utility data to simulate baseline. | Major refrigeration plant redesign, evaporator replacement. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The choice of option depends on the **size, complexity, and risk** of the ECM. For most food industry ECMs, **Option A or B** is appropriate.

---

#### **Part (b) — Motor Replacement Numerical [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- Old motor efficiency: $\eta_1 = 88\% = 0.88$
- New motor efficiency: $\eta_2 = 93.5\% = 0.935$
- Motor rated shaft power: $P_{\text{shaft}} = 22 \; \text{kW}$
- Operating hours: $t = 4{,}800 \; \text{h/year}$
- Electricity cost: $C_e = ₹8.00/\text{kWh}$
- New motor cost: $C_0 = ₹95{,}000$

**Step 1: Input power to old motor**

$$
P_{\text{in,1}} = \frac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{\eta_1} = \frac{22}{0.88} = 25.00 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2: Input power to new motor**

$$
P_{\text{in,2}} = \frac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{\eta_2} = \frac{22}{0.935} = 23.53 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3: Power saving**

$$
\Delta P = 25.00 - 23.53 = 1.47 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4: Annual energy savings**

$$
\Delta E = \Delta P \times t = 1.47 \times 4{,}800 = 7{,}082 \; \text{kWh/year}
$$

**Step 5: Annual monetary savings**

$$
\text{MS} = \Delta E \times C_e = 7{,}082 \times 8.00 = ₹56{,}656 \; \text{per year}
$$

**Step 6: Simple Payback Period (SPP)**

$$
\text{SPP} = \frac{C_0}{\text{MS}} = \frac{95{,}000}{56{,}656} = 1.677 \; \text{years} \approx 1 \; \text{year and 8 \; months}
$$

**Step 7: ROI (per year)**

$$
\text{ROI} = \frac{56{,}656}{95{,}000} \times 100 = 59.6\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The motor replacement is **highly justified** — payback in under 2 years and an annual ROI of nearly 60%, which far exceeds typical corporate hurdle rates (10%–15%).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (EnMS Questions) — READ CAREFULLY:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize energy** before calculating savings. If a plant increased production by 30% but absolute energy dropped by only 5%, the *un-normalized* % appears small — the *normalized* % may actually be negative. Always show both.
> 2. **Confusing EnB with total energy consumed** in the baseline year. EnB can be expressed as absolute (GJ) OR specific (GJ/unit), and KTU examiners expect the *specific* form for cross-year comparison.
> 3. **Skipping the re-baseline analysis** in numerical problems. ISO 50006 *mandates* re-baselining when production changes by > ± 20%, fuel type changes, or major equipment is added. This is a **3-mark differentiator** between average and top answers.
> 4. **Drawing PDCA without clause references.** Mentioning ISO 50001:2018 clauses (4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10) attached to each PDCA phase is essential — without this, you lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **In M&V numericals, omitting M&V Option (A/B/C/D) justification.** Examiners reward students who explicitly state *which* M&V option applies and *why*.
> 6. **Mixing up "Energy Efficiency" and "Energy Performance."** Energy efficiency is the ratio of useful output to energy input; EnP (per ISO 50001) is the broader measurable result covering efficiency, use, and consumption.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points — KTU Module 3, EnMS & Standards:**

- ✅ **EnMS** = systematic framework → governed by **ISO 50001:2018**.
- ✅ **PDCA Cycle** is the spine: **Plan (clauses 4–7.1) → Do (clause 8) → Check (clause 9) → Act (clause 10)**.
- ✅ **Energy Baseline (EnB)** is a *static* quantitative reference, set for a representative historical period, used to compare energy performance over time.
- ✅ **EnPI** is a *dynamic* ratio: EnPI = Energy ÷ Production Variable (e.g., GJ/tonne, kWh/kL, MJ/kg).
- ✅ **Normalize first, then compare** — this is the only way to isolate efficiency improvement from production-volume effect.
- ✅ **% Energy Savings** = $\frac{E_{\text{normalized,current}} - E_{\text{actual,current}}}{E_{\text{normalized,current}}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Re-baseline triggers** (ISO 50006): production change > ± 20%, fuel change, major equipment change, operating hours > ± 10%, or product mix shift > 15%.
- ✅ **Supporting standards to memorize:**
  - **ISO 50002** — Energy Audit requirements
  - **ISO 50004** — Implementation guidance
  - **ISO 50006** — EnB & EnPI measurement
  - **ISO 50015** — Measurement & Verification (M&V)
  - **ISO 50047** — Energy savings determination
- ✅ **IPMVP Options A / B / C / D** — know when to use which (key parameter / all parameters / whole facility / calibrated simulation).
- ✅ **Energy Conversion Factors to remember:** 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 0.0036 GJ; 1 toe = 41.868 GJ = 11,630 kWh; 1 kcal = 4.186 kJ.
- ✅ **M&V procedure (ISO 50015):** M&V Plan → Install/Use Meters → Baseline Data → Post-Retrofit Data → Normalize → Calculate Savings → Report → Re-verify.
- ✅ **ISO 50001:2018** is built on **Annex SL**, so it integrates with **ISO 9001 (Quality)** and **ISO 14001 (Environment)** into a single IMS.
- ✅ Typical energy savings: **10%–30%** within 2–3 years of EnMS implementation (IEA estimate).
- ✅ **Payback Period** = Investment ÷ Annual Savings (years); **ROI** = (Annual Savings ÷ Investment) × 100%.
- ✅ **Affinity Laws** for VFD/fan/pump: $P \propto Q^3$, $P \propto N^3$, $Q \propto N$ (memorize for numerical questions).
- ✅ **Boiler efficiency** (direct method): $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{steam}}(h_g - h_f)}{m_{\text{fuel}} \times \text{GCV}} \times 100$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of ISO 50001:2018 Standard for Energy Management Systems (EnMS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INDUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Overview of ISO 50001:2018 Standard for Energy Management Systems (EnMS) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **ISO 50001:2018** is the second edition of the international standard titled *"Energy management systems — Requirements with guidance for use,"* published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** in August 2018. It supersedes the first edition (ISO 50001:2011). The standard specifies the requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and improving an **Energy Management System (EnMS)**, the intended outcome of which is to enable an organization to take a **systematic approach** to achieving continual improvement of **energy performance** and the **energy management system**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Definition (KTU Board Standard):** > **Energy Management System (EnMS)** is a set of interrelated or interacting elements to establish an energy policy and energy objectives, and the processes and procedures to achieve those objectives. > [!NOTE] > **Why ISO 50001:2018 matters in the Food Industry:** > The food processing industry is one of the most energy-intensive manufacturing sectors — using significant thermal energy for **boiling, pasteurization, sterilization, drying, baking**, and electrical energy for **refrigeration, cold storage, milling, pumping, and packaging**. ISO 50001:2018 provides the *structural roadmap* to systematically reduce this energy intensity while maintaining product quality and food safety. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have decided to **lose 10 kg of body weight** in 6 months. What would you do? 1. You would **measure** your current weight (Energy Baseline). 2. You would **plan** a diet and exercise routine (Energy Objectives & Action Plans). 3. You would **do** — actually follow the plan (Implementation & Operation). 4. You would **check** your progress every week by weighing yourself (Monitoring, Measurement & Analysis). 5. You would **act** on what you learned — adjust the diet if not working (Continual Improvement). 6. You would also get a **doctor's checkup** every quarter to make sure your plan is healthy (Internal Audit & Management Review). **ISO 50001:2018 works exactly the same way — but for the "energy health" of a factory, bakery, dairy plant, or food processing unit.** Instead of a weighing scale, the organization uses **meters and Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)**. Instead of a diet plan, it uses an **Energy Action Plan**. Instead of a doctor, it uses an **internal auditor and top management review**. > [!TIP] > **One-line memory hook for exams:** > "**ISO 50001:2018 is the PDCA wheel that turns the EnMS bicycle of an organization toward lower energy use and better energy performance.**" --- ### 1.3 Core Terminology You Must Memorize | Term | Definition (as per ISO 50001:2018) | |---|---| | **Energy Performance** | Measurable result(s) related to energy efficiency, energy use, and energy consumption. | | **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** | Quantitative value or measure of energy performance as defined by the organization. | | **Energy Baseline (EnB)** | Quantitative reference(s) providing a basis for comparison of energy performance. | | **Significant Energy Use (SEU)** | Energy use accounting for substantial energy consumption and/or offering considerable potential for energy performance improvement. | | **Energy Review** | Determination of the organization's energy profile, including Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs), based on data and other information, identification of opportunities for improvement. | | **Energy Objective** | Result or outcome specified by the organization's energy policy that is to be achieved. | | **Energy Target** | Quantified value or metric of an energy objective. | | **Energy Management System (EnMS)** | Management system used to establish and achieve energy objectives and energy targets. | | **HLS / Harmonized Structure** | The 10-clause common structure shared by all modern ISO management system standards. | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet ### 2.1 The Big Picture: The PDCA Cycle at the Heart of ISO 50001:2018 ISO 50001:2018 is built on the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA) cycle**, also called the **Deming Cycle**. This is the same engine that drives ISO 9001 (Quality) and ISO 14001 (Environment). The reason is that ISO has aligned all modern management standards to a single **10-clause Harmonized Structure (HLS)**. > [!NOTE] > **PDCA applied to EnMS:** > - **Plan** → Establish the energy policy, objectives, targets, action plans, energy review, EnPIs, and EnB. > - **Do** → Implement the action plans, train people, operate controls, manage SEUs. > - **Check** → Monitor, measure, analyze energy performance; conduct internal audits. > - **Act** → Management review, take corrective actions, drive continual improvement. --- ### 2.2 The 10 Clauses of ISO 50001:2018 (HLS Structure) | Clause | Title | PDCA Stage | Core Requirement | |:---:|---|:---:|---| | 1 | Scope | — | Defines applicability of the standard. | | 2 | Normative References | — | Refers to ISO/IEC directives. | | 3 | Terms and Definitions | — | Vocabulary used. | | 4 | **Context of the Organization** | **Plan** | Identify internal/external issues, needs of interested parties, scope of EnMS. | | 5 | **Leadership** | **Plan** | Top management commitment, energy policy, roles & responsibilities. | | 6 | **Planning** | **Plan** | Risks & opportunities, energy review, SEUs, EnPIs, EnB, objectives, action plans. | | 7 | **Support** | **Do** | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information. | | 8 | **Operation** | **Do** | Operational planning & control, design, procurement, SEU management. | | 9 | **Performance Evaluation** | **Check** | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, evaluation of compliance, internal audit, management review. | | 10 | **Improvement** | **Act** | Nonconformity, corrective action, continual improvement. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Clauses 4–10 are auditable (the actual EnMS requirements). Clauses 1–3 are introductory. Any KTU question asking for "the structure of ISO 50001" almost always expects this 10-clause table.** --- ### 2.3 Key Concept: The Energy Review (Clause 6.3) The **Energy Review** is the *single most important planning activity* in ISO 50001:2018. It involves: 1. **Analysis of energy use and consumption** based on measured data. 2. **Identification of SEUs** — energy uses with high consumption or high improvement potential. 3. **Determination of EnPIs** — suitable performance indicators for each SEU. 4. **Establishment of EnB(s)** — the reference baseline(s). 5. **Identification of opportunities for improvement** and prioritization. --- ### 2.4 The EnPI–EnB–Energy Performance Relationship (The Core Mathematical Engine) Energy performance is **not** just "lower kWh." It is the relationship between energy consumption and a relevant **normalizing variable** (e.g., tonnes of product, hours of operation, °C-day of cooling load). The simplest normalized EnPI formula is: $$ \text{EnPI} = \frac{\text{Energy Consumption}}{\text{Production Output}} $$ > [!TIP] > **Food industry example:** In a dairy plant, the EnPI is often **kWh per tonne of milk processed** or **MJ per kg of pasteurized milk**. The EnB is the value of this EnPI during a defined baseline period (e.g., the average of 2022–2023). When multiple variables affect energy use (e.g., ambient temperature, throughput, raw material moisture), a **linear regression model** is used to normalize the EnPI: $$ E = a + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2 + \ldots + b_n x_n $$ where: - $E$ = energy consumption in the baseline period, - $a$ = baseline constant, - $b_i$ = regression coefficient for variable $x_i$, - $x_i$ = normalizing variable (e.g., production volume, degree-days). The **Energy Performance Improvement** is then calculated as: $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{\text{EnPI}_{\text{baseline period}} - \text{EnPI}_{\text{reporting period}}}{\text{EnPI}_{\text{baseline period}}} \times 100 $$ --- ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Concept Cheat Sheet | Concept | One-line Meaning | Food Industry Example | |---|---|---| | **EnMS** | The total management system for energy. | Whole-company energy policy of an F&B manufacturer. | | **Energy Policy** | Top-level commitment statement. | "Reduce specific energy consumption by 10% in 3 years." | | **Energy Review** | First-time deep analysis of energy. | Mapping every boiler, chiller, oven, motor. | | **SEU** | Energy uses worth tracking. | Steam boiler, ammonia refrigeration plant, hot oil heater. | | **EnPI** | The metric to track energy performance. | MJ / kg of bakery product; kWh / m³ of cold storage. | | **EnB** | The reference point for EnPI. | Average EnPI of FY 2022–2023. | | **Energy Objective** | What you want to achieve. | "Install VFD on cooling tower pumps." | | **Energy Target** | The numeric value of the objective. | "Achieve 0.85 kWh/tonne-cooling by FY 2026." | | **PDCA** | The cycle that drives continual improvement. | Plan a project → Execute → Measure → Improve. | | **HLS** | The 10-clause structure of all modern ISO MSS. | Same as ISO 9001 / ISO 14001 clause structure. | | **Continual Improvement** | Recurring process of enhancing EnPI. | Year-on-year reduction in kWh/tonne. | | **Internal Audit** | Self-check against the standard. | Annual audit by trained internal auditors. | | **Management Review** | Top management evaluates the EnMS. | Quarterly review by MD/Plant Head. | | **Energy Action Plan** | Documented plan to achieve objectives. | Action plan with deadline, owner, budget. | | **Normalizing Variable** | Variable used to make EnPI comparable. | Production volume, degree-day, humidity. | --- ### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering In a food industry context, ISO 50001:2018: - Provides a **structured methodology** to reduce Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in high-load areas like **dairy, brewing, sugar, bakery, edible oil, and meat processing**. - Enables **integration with ISO 14001 (Environment)** and **ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000 (Food Safety)** — a single audit, one management system, reduced cost. - Qualifies an organization for **government incentives, tax rebates, and carbon credits** in many jurisdictions. - Is a **legal/contractual enabler** — many multinational buyers demand ISO 50001 certification from suppliers. - Aligns with **ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance)** reporting, increasingly required by stock exchanges and global buyers. - Facilitates **Net-Zero roadmaps** and **Scope 1, 2, 3 GHG accounting**. > [!NOTE] > **Why this is a high-yield KTU topic:** The food industry is energy-intensive, and the course itself (*Energy Management in Food Industry*) is essentially an applied ISO 50001 module. Expect a 14-mark question linking EnPI/EnB/SEU to a real food plant scenario. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough: EnMS Establishment & EnPI Normalization ### 3.1 The Step-by-Step Process of Establishing an EnMS in a Food Plant Below is the **exhaustive 12-step roadmap** a food processing organization must follow to implement ISO 50001:2018. Each step is mapped to the relevant clause. **Step 1 — Define the Context and Scope (Clause 4):** Identify the physical/organizational boundary (e.g., "Dairy Plant A, including reception, pasteurization, homogenization, packaging, and cold storage, but excluding the administration block and outsourced logistics"). **Step 2 — Identify Interested Parties and Their Needs (Clause 4.2):** These may include: top management, employees, statutory bodies (Pollution Control Board, Electrical Inspector), customers (who demand low-carbon products), suppliers of energy, and the community. **Step 3 — Determine the Scope and Boundaries of the EnMS (Clause 4.3):** Define what is *in scope* (e.g., boiler house, refrigeration plant, compressed air) and what is *out of scope* (e.g., residential colony of the company). **Step 4 — Establish the Energy Policy (Clause 5.2):** The Managing Director signs a one-page policy committing to: - Energy performance improvement. - Provision of information and resources. - Compliance with legal and other requirements. - Continual improvement. **Step 5 — Assign Roles, Responsibilities, and Authorities (Clause 5.3):** Appoint a **Management Representative (MR)** — usually the Chief Engineer — and an **Energy Manager** with a cross-functional Energy Team. **Step 6 — Conduct the Energy Review (Clause 6.3):** - Collect at least 12 months of energy data (electricity bills, fuel bills, steam meter logs). - Calculate Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) for every process. - Identify **SEUs**. - Determine initial **EnPIs** and **EnB**. **Step 7 — Identify and Evaluate Risks & Opportunities (Clause 6.1):** - Risks: increasing electricity tariff, equipment failure, fuel supply disruption. - Opportunities: VFD installation, waste heat recovery, solar PV, biomass boiler. **Step 8 — Establish Energy Objectives and Targets (Clause 6.4):** - Objective: "Reduce specific energy consumption in pasteurization." - Target: "From 0.45 kWh/tonne to 0.38 kWh/tonne within 24 months." **Step 9 — Develop Energy Action Plans (Clause 6.5):** Each target gets a plan with: actions, resources, responsibilities, timeline, and method of verification. **Step 10 — Implement and Operate (Clause 8):** - Operational controls for SEUs. - Maintenance schedules. - Calibration of meters. - Design and procurement criteria for energy-efficient equipment. **Step 11 — Check — Performance Evaluation (Clause 9):** - Monitor and measure EnPIs. - Evaluate legal compliance. - Conduct internal audits. - Conduct management review. **Step 12 — Act — Improvement (Clause 10):** - Address nonconformities. - Take corrective actions. - Drive continual improvement. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: EnPI Normalization Using Linear Regression **Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark style):** > A dairy plant records the following monthly data for its **pasteurization section** for the *baseline year (2023)*. The plant wants to establish an **Energy Baseline (EnB)** and an **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** for the section. | Month | Energy $E$ (MWh) | Production $P$ (tonnes) | Avg. ambient temp $T$ (°C) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Jan | 320 | 1100 | 18 | | Feb | 305 | 1080 | 20 | | Mar | 340 | 1200 | 24 | | Apr | 360 | 1280 | 28 | | May | 395 | 1350 | 32 | | Jun | 420 | 1400 | 35 | | Jul | 445 | 1420 | 36 | | Aug | 440 | 1400 | 35 | | Sep | 400 | 1320 | 31 | | Oct | 360 | 1250 | 28 | | Nov | 330 | 1150 | 22 | | Dec | 310 | 1100 | 19 | A simple **linear regression model** is proposed: $$ E = a + b_1 P + b_2 T $$ Using least-squares regression (or, in exam practice, assuming the regression has already been solved and the coefficients are given), the result is: $$ a = 50.0, \quad b_1 = 0.20, \quad b_2 = 2.50 $$ So the regression model is: $$ E = 50.0 + 0.20 \cdot P + 2.50 \cdot T $$ Units: - $E$ in MWh, - $P$ in tonnes of milk, - $T$ in °C, - $a$ in MWh (intercept), - $b_1$ in MWh per tonne, - $b_2$ in MWh per °C. **Now, in the reporting year (2024), one particular month (say, May 2024) has:** - Actual energy consumed: $E_{\text{actual}} = 380$ MWh, - Production: $P = 1380$ tonnes, - Avg. ambient temp: $T = 33$ °C. **Step 1 — Calculate the expected (baseline-model-predicted) energy:** $$ E_{\text{expected}} = 50.0 + (0.20)(1380) + (2.50)(33) $$ $$ E_{\text{expected}} = 50.0 + 276.0 + 82.5 $$ $$ E_{\text{expected}} = 408.5 \text{ MWh} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate the adjusted energy performance (Energy Performance Improvement %):** The **% improvement in energy performance** is calculated by: $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{E_{\text{expected}} - E_{\text{actual}}}{E_{\text{expected}}} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{408.5 - 380}{408.5} \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{28.5}{408.5} \times 100 \approx 6.98\% $$ > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** Because $E_{\text{actual}} < E_{\text{expected}}$, the plant consumed **less energy than the baseline model predicted** for the same production and ambient conditions. This is a **6.98 % energy performance improvement** — the plant has genuinely improved, not merely produced less. **Step 3 — Verify by checking the EnPI (specific energy consumption):** $$ \text{EnPI}_{\text{baseline}} = \frac{E_{\text{expected}}}{P} = \frac{408.5}{1380} = 0.296 \text{ MWh/tonne} $$ $$ \text{EnPI}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{E_{\text{actual}}}{P} = \frac{380}{1380} = 0.275 \text{ MWh/tonne} $$ $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{0.296 - 0.275}{0.296} \times 100 \approx 7.09\% $$ The small difference between 6.98 % and 7.09 % arises from rounding. Both methods are correct and accepted under ISO 50001:2018. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why two methods?** > - **Method 1** (expected vs actual energy) accounts for the effect of the normalizing variables ($P$ and $T$) on energy. > - **Method 2** (EnPI ratio) is simpler and acceptable when the EnPI is well-chosen. > In KTU answers, **show both** for full marks. --- ### 3.3 Common KTU Pitfalls in EnPI Calculations (and how to avoid them) | Pitfall | Why it loses marks | Correct approach | |---|---|---| | Using **total energy** as the EnPI. | Ignores changes in production volume. | Use **specific energy** (energy / production) as EnPI. | | **Forgetting normalizing variables** (e.g., ambient temp for cooling load). | Overstates or understates improvement. | Use **regression** or **CUSUM** techniques. | | Computing improvement on a **non-representative baseline**. | A bad month becomes the comparison point. | Use **12-month rolling average** or **average of 3 years**. | | **Conflating energy use reduction with energy performance improvement.** | Energy use can drop simply because production dropped. | Energy performance must be **normalized**. | | Using **one EnPI for the whole plant** when SEUs differ. | A single EnPI hides poor performance in some areas. | Define **separate EnPIs for each SEU**. | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 PDCA Cycle Mapped to ISO 50001:2018 Clauses ```mermaid flowchart LR A["PLAN
Clause 4 Context
Clause 5 Leadership
Clause 6 Planning"] --> B["DO
Clause 7 Support
Clause 8 Operation"] B --> C["CHECK
Clause 9 Performance Evaluation"] C --> D["ACT
Clause 10 Improvement"] D --> A A:::plan B:::do C:::check D:::act classDef plan fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000 classDef do fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 classDef check fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 classDef act fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,color:#000 ``` --- ### 4.2 EnMS Architecture: From Policy to Continual Improvement ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Energy Policy
Top Management Commitment"] --> R["Roles and Responsibilities
MR, Energy Manager, Team"] R --> ER["Energy Review
Data Collection and Analysis"] ER --> SEU["Identify Significant Energy Uses SEU"] SEU --> ENPI["Define EnPIs and EnB"] ENPI --> OBJ["Energy Objectives and Targets"] OBJ --> AP["Energy Action Plans"] AP --> OP["Operation and Operational Controls"] OP --> MON["Monitoring and Measurement"] MON --> AUD["Internal Audit"] AUD --> MR2["Management Review"] MR2 --> CI["Continual Improvement"] CI --> OBJ ER2["Risks and Opportunities"] --> AP classDef policy fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 classDef review fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000 classDef act fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 classDef monitor fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000 class P,R policy class ER,SEU,ENPI,OBJ,AP,ER2 review class OP,MR2,CI act class MON,AUD monitor ``` --- ### 4.3 EnPI/EnB/SEU Relationship Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR EU["Energy Use
kWh, MJ, m3 gas"] --> SEU1["SEU 1
Boiler"] EU --> SEU2["SEU 2
Refrigeration"] EU --> SEU3["SEU 3
Compressed Air"] SEU1 --> ENPI1["EnPI 1
MJ per kg steam"] SEU2 --> ENPI2["EnPI 2
kWh per tonne cooling"] SEU3 --> ENPI3["EnPI 3
m3 per kWh generated"] ENPI1 --> ENB1["EnB 1
Baseline value of EnPI 1"] ENPI2 --> ENB2["EnB 2
Baseline value of EnPI 2"] ENPI3 --> ENB3["EnB 3
Baseline value of EnPI 3"] ENB1 --> CI["Compare to EnB
Compute Improvement"] ENB2 --> CI ENB3 --> CI classDef seuc fill:#FFECB3,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000 classDef enpic fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,color:#000 classDef enbc fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,color:#000 classDef resultc fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 class SEU1,SEU2,SEU3 seuc class ENPI1,ENPI2,ENPI3 enpic class ENB1,ENB2,ENB3 enbc class CI resultc ``` --- ### 4.4 ISO 50001:2018 Integration with Other Food Industry Standards ```mermaid flowchart TB ISO50001["ISO 50001:2018
Energy Management"] --> ISO14001["ISO 14001:2015
Environmental Management"] ISO50001 --> ISO9001["ISO 9001:2015
Quality Management"] ISO50001 --> ISO22000["ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000
Food Safety Management"] ISO50001 --> ISO45001["ISO 45001:2018
Occupational Health and Safety"] ISO14001 --> HLS["Common HLS Framework
10 Clause Structure"] ISO9001 --> HLS ISO22000 --> HLS ISO45001 --> HLS HLS --> IMS["Integrated Management System IMS
Single Audit, Lower Cost, Holistic View"] classDef en fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 classDef fs fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 classDef hls fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000 classDef ims fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000 class ISO50001,ISO14001,ISO9001 en class ISO22000,ISO45001 fs class HLS hls class IMS ims ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **Define the term "Energy Management System (EnMS)" as per ISO 50001:2018. List any four elements of an EnMS.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > An **Energy Management System (EnMS)** is a set of interrelated or interacting elements used by an organization to establish an energy policy and energy objectives, and the processes and procedures to achieve those objectives (definition – 1.5 marks). > > Four elements of an EnMS are (0.5 × 4 = 2 marks, list any four): > 1. Energy policy > 2. Energy objectives and targets > 3. Energy review (including SEUs, EnPIs, EnB) > 4. Energy action plans > 5. Operational controls and procedures > 6. Monitoring, measurement, and analysis > 7. Internal audit and management review > 8. Continual improvement process --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]** **Distinguish between EnPI and EnB. Give one example from the food industry.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | EnPI (Energy Performance Indicator) | EnB (Energy Baseline) | |---|---|---| | Meaning | Quantitative **measure** of energy performance. | Quantitative **reference value** providing a basis for comparison. | | Role | Tracks energy performance over time. | The benchmark against which EnPI is compared. | | Example (Dairy) | **kWh per tonne of pasteurized milk.** | **0.42 kWh/tonne** (the average of FY 2022–2023). | (2 marks for distinction + 1 mark for example.) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice > **Module 3 Choice Question (14 marks)** > Attempt **any one** of the following: --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the **PDCA cycle** as applied to ISO 50001:2018. Map each PDCA stage to the relevant clauses of the standard. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **10-clause Harmonized Structure (HLS)** of ISO 50001:2018. Why is this structure important for integrating EnMS with other ISO management systems in a food industry? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) PDCA Cycle in ISO 50001:2018 (7 marks):** The **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA) cycle** is the foundational management methodology of ISO 50001:2018. It ensures a structured and repeatable approach to energy performance improvement (1 mark). | PDCA Stage | Activity in EnMS | ISO 50001 Clause | |---|---|---| | **Plan** | Establish energy policy, conduct energy review, identify SEUs, determine EnPIs and EnB, set objectives and targets, develop action plans. | Clauses **4, 5, 6** (Context, Leadership, Planning). | | **Do** | Provide resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information; implement operational controls, design, procurement. | Clauses **7, 8** (Support, Operation). | | **Check** | Monitor & measure energy performance, evaluate compliance, conduct internal audit, conduct management review. | Clause **9** (Performance Evaluation). | | **Act** | Address nonconformities, take corrective action, drive continual improvement. | Clause **10** (Improvement). | (1 mark per row + 1 mark for explanation of iterative nature = 7 marks.) > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's pitfall callout:** Many students *list* PDCA but forget to **map the clauses explicitly**. That costs 2–3 marks. Always include a clause-to-stage mapping table. **(b) Harmonized Structure (HLS) of ISO 50001:2018 (7 marks):** The **Harmonized Structure (HLS)** — sometimes called Annex SL — is a **10-clause template** adopted by ISO for all its management system standards (MSS). ISO 50001:2018, ISO 9001:2015, ISO 14001:2015, and ISO 45001:2018 all use the *same* 10-clause structure (1 mark). **The 10 Clauses (2 marks):** 1. Scope 2. Normative References 3. Terms and Definitions 4. Context of the Organization 5. Leadership 6. Planning 7. Support 8. Operation 9. Performance Evaluation 10. Improvement **Importance for food industry integration (4 marks):** 1. **Single audit, lower cost** — A dairy or bakery unit can integrate ISO 50001 with ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000 (food safety) and ISO 14001 (environment) and conduct a single combined audit. This saves 30–40 % of certification cost. 2. **Unified documentation** — Common structure means common procedures, common forms, common manuals. Reduces paperwork for a food plant where documentation is already heavy (FSSAI, HACCP, etc.). 3. **Holistic improvement** — Energy, environment, quality, safety, and food safety become a **single Integrated Management System (IMS)**. Decisions (e.g., changing a refrigerant) can simultaneously address GHG reduction, ozone depletion, energy efficiency, and food safety. 4. **Common language for top management** — The HLS forces all standards to use identical terms like "context," "risks and opportunities," "leadership," "documented information." A single Management Review can cover all systems. 5. **Easier continual improvement** — Since all systems follow PDCA, a single corrective action can address energy, quality, environment, and safety issues at once. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's pitfall callout:** Students often write "HLS has 7 clauses" or "HLS is in ISO 14001 only" — both are wrong. State clearly: **HLS has 10 clauses, applies to all modern ISO MSS, and was introduced via ISO/IEC Directives Part 1 Annex SL.** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3]** **(a)** Define **SEU, EnPI, and EnB**. Explain the **Energy Review** process as required by Clause 6.3 of ISO 50001:2018. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A food processing unit has the following data for its **refrigeration section** for the baseline year: - Total energy consumed (E) = 1200 MWh, - Total production = 8000 tonnes of frozen product, - Energy Baseline (EnB) = 0.15 MWh/tonne. In the reporting year, with production = 8500 tonnes and energy consumed = 1180 MWh: 1. Calculate the **EnPI for both years** and the **% improvement** in energy performance. 2. Explain whether this reflects a **genuine energy performance improvement**. If not, suggest the correct method to assess improvement when production and other variables change. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Definitions + Energy Review (7 marks):** **Definitions (3 marks = 1 mark each):** - **Significant Energy Use (SEU):** An energy use that accounts for substantial energy consumption and/or offers considerable potential for energy performance improvement. - **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI):** A quantitative value or measure of energy performance, as defined by the organization (e.g., kWh/tonne, MJ/kg). - **Energy Baseline (EnB):** A quantitative reference providing a basis for comparison of energy performance. **Energy Review Process (Clause 6.3) — 4 marks:** 1. **Analysis of energy use and consumption** — collect historical data (minimum 12 months), map all energy sources (electricity, fuel oil, LPG, biomass, steam). 2. **Identification of SEUs** — rank energy uses by consumption and improvement potential. 3. **Determination of EnPIs** — select appropriate EnPIs for each SEU, including normalizing variables (production, throughput, ambient temperature, humidity). 4. **Establishment of EnB(s)** — define the baseline period (e.g., average of last 3 years or a representative year) and document the baseline EnPI values. 5. **Identification of opportunities for improvement** — list energy-saving opportunities, rank them by payback period, technical feasibility, and energy-saving potential. (Allocate 0.8 mark per step.) > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's pitfall callout:** Do **not** write a generic "energy audit" procedure. ISO 50001:2018 calls it the **Energy Review**, and it must include **SEU identification + EnPI selection + EnB establishment + opportunity identification**. Missing any one of these 4 elements costs 1 mark. **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Calculate EnPI for baseline year (1 mark):** $$ \text{EnPI}_{\text{baseline}} = \frac{E_{\text{baseline}}}{P_{\text{baseline}}} = \frac{1200 \text{ MWh}}{8000 \text{ tonnes}} = 0.150 \text{ MWh/tonne} $$ (This equals the EnB, as expected.) **Step 2 — Calculate EnPI for reporting year (1 mark):** $$ \text{EnPI}_{\text{reporting}} = \frac{E_{\text{reporting}}}{P_{\text{reporting}}} = \frac{1180 \text{ MWh}}{8500 \text{ tonnes}} = 0.1388 \text{ MWh/tonne} $$ **Step 3 — Calculate % improvement (1 mark):** $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{\text{EnPI}_{\text{baseline}} - \text{EnPI}_{\text{reporting}}}{\text{EnPI}_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100 $$ $$ = \frac{0.150 - 0.1388}{0.150} \times 100 = \frac{0.0112}{0.150} \times 100 \approx 7.47\% $$ **Step 4 — Is this genuine improvement? (2 marks):** > **Yes, this is a genuine improvement.** The EnPI has dropped from 0.150 to 0.1388 MWh/tonne. Importantly, the production **increased** from 8000 to 8500 tonnes, yet the absolute energy consumption **decreased** from 1200 to 1180 MWh. Both indicators point to the same conclusion — the refrigeration section has become more energy-efficient per tonne of product. **Step 5 — Method when multiple variables change (2 marks):** If, in a different scenario, production had decreased (say, 7000 tonnes → 6000 tonnes) and energy had also decreased, the **simple EnPI would falsely show improvement**. In that case, ISO 50001:2018 recommends: 1. **Use multiple normalizing variables** in the EnPI — e.g., kWh per tonne AND kWh per degree-day of cooling load. 2. **Use a linear regression model** to predict expected energy at the new operating conditions, and then compare actual energy with predicted energy: $$ E_{\text{expected}} = a + b_1 P + b_2 T $$ $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{E_{\text{expected}} - E_{\text{actual}}}{E_{\text{expected}}} \times 100 $$ 3. **Use CUSUM (cumulative sum) techniques** for tracking persistent changes. 4. **Re-baseline** if there is a permanent change in process, equipment, or product mix — but **only** after documenting the change and re-establishing the EnB. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's pitfall callout (numerical):** Two very common mistakes: > 1. Students **subtract EnPIs directly** (0.150 − 0.1388 = 0.0112) and call that the "improvement." The correct method is to **divide by the baseline EnPI** to get a *percentage*. A 0.0112 MWh/tonne improvement means different things at different baselines. > 2. Students ignore the production increase and call it a "raw energy reduction." Always emphasize **specific** energy, not absolute energy. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **The 15 must-remember points for ISO 50001:2018 in the food industry:** - [x] **ISO 50001:2018** is the **second edition**, published in **August 2018**, replacing ISO 50001:2011. - [x] It is built on the **PDCA cycle** (Deming cycle) and the **10-clause Harmonized Structure (HLS)**. - [x] **Clauses 4–10 are the auditable requirements**; Clauses 1–3 are introductory. - [x] **Plan** = Clauses 4, 5, 6. **Do** = Clauses 7, 8. **Check** = Clause 9. **Act** = Clause 10. - [x] **EnMS** = Energy Management System — the totality of the management system for energy. - [x] **Energy Policy** must be signed by **top management** (a Clause 5 leadership requirement). - [x] **Energy Review** (Clause 6.3) is the foundation of the entire EnMS — it produces **SEUs, EnPIs, and EnB**. - [x] **SEU** = Significant Energy Use — the energy uses worth tracking and managing. - [x] **EnPI** = Energy Performance Indicator — the *metric* (e.g., kWh/tonne, MJ/kg). - [x] **EnB** = Energy Baseline — the *reference value* of the EnPI in the baseline period. - [x] Energy performance = **normalized energy use** — never just absolute kWh. - [x] The standard allows **regression-based normalization** for multiple variables: $$ E = a + b_1 x_1 + b_2 x_2 + \ldots + b_n x_n $$ - [x] Improvement % is calculated as: $$ \text{Improvement (\%)} = \frac{E_{\text{expected}} - E_{\text{actual}}}{E_{\text{expected}}} \times 100 $$ - [x] ISO 50001:2018 **integrates seamlessly** with **ISO 9001, ISO 14001, ISO 45001, and ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000** through the common HLS — a major advantage for food industry. - [x] Food industry SEUs typically include: **steam boiler, refrigeration plant, compressed air, electric motors, ovens, dryers, and cold storage**. - [x] **Continual improvement** is the *intended outcome* of the entire EnMS — measured by a sustained year-on-year reduction in EnPIs. - [x] **Internal audit + Management Review** (Clause 9) are mandatory checkpoints, and **nonconformity + corrective action** (Clause 10) are mandatory improvement mechanisms. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-mile memory hook for the exam hall:** > *"**Policy → Review → SEU/EnPI/EnB → Objectives/Targets → Action Plans → Operate → Measure → Audit → Review → Improve.*** > *This is the **10-step arrow of ISO 50001:2018**. Trace it once on your answer sheet before writing the answer."*

topics.title: Implementation Steps and Requirements for EnMS in Food Processing Units
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INSUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Implementation Steps and Requirements for EnMS in Food Processing Units ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Energy Management System (EnMS)** in a food processing unit is a structured, documented, and continuously improved set of interrelated management practices, procedures, processes, and resources that establishes an **energy policy**, sets **energy objectives and targets**, identifies **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)**, and implements operational controls to reduce Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) across thermal, electrical, and refrigeration energy streams in food manufacturing. The international benchmark for EnMS is **ISO 50001:2018** — *Energy management systems — Requirements with guidance for use*, which is built on the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle and the **High-Level Structure (HLS)** of ISO management standards. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For food processing units, an EnMS is **not** a software package — it is a *management discipline* that binds boilers, steam networks, chillers, compressors, dryers, evaporators, and process lines under a single auditable energy-governance framework. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Diet Plan" of a Factory** > Think of an EnMS exactly like a personalized diet plan a doctor gives a patient. > - The **doctor (Top Management)** writes a *Health Policy* — eat less sugar, more protein. > - A **blood test (Initial Energy Review)** reveals where the body's energy is leaking. > - A **weight baseline** is fixed: "You weigh 85 kg today." > - **KPIs** (kilograms lost per week, calories per day) become the *EnPIs*. > - **Monthly weigh-ins** are the *Monitoring & Measurement* stage. > - **Doctor reviews** every 3 months are the *Management Review*. > If the patient slips, the plan is *corrected* — the cycle never ends. > > A food factory is the *patient*, the **kWh of steam, electricity, and refrigeration** are the *calories*, and the **PDCA cycle is the doctor's prescription**. --- ### 1.3 Why an EnMS is Non-Negotiable in Food Processing Food processing is one of the **most energy-intensive** industrial sectors because it combines: | Energy Stream | Typical Unit in Food Industry | Why It Matters | |---|---|---| | **Thermal (Steam)** | ton of steam per ton of product | Pasteurization, sterilization, evaporation, boiling | | **Electrical** | kWh per ton of product | Motors, pumps, fans, conveyors, lighting | | **Refrigeration** | TR (Ton of Refrigeration) | Cold storage, chilling, freezing, IQF | | **Compressed Air** | Nm³/min | Pneumatic conveying, cleaning, packaging | | **Hot Water / Process Heat** | MJ/kg of product | CIP (Cleaning-In-Place), blanching, cooking | > [!NOTE] > **Energy Intensity Benchmark (Bureau of Indian Energy Efficiency standards):** > - Dairy processing: **100–250 kWh/ton of milk processed** > - Sugar industry: **28–35 kWh/ton of cane crushed** > - Rice milling: **55–70 kWh/ton of paddy** > - Cold storage: **80–120 kWh/ton-month** These are the *reference numbers* a KTU examiner expects you to recall. --- ### 1.4 Standard Reference Framework The globally accepted framework is **ISO 50001:2018**, which contains **10 clauses (Clauses 4–10)** and uses the HLS alignment common to ISO 9001, ISO 14001, and ISO 45001. - **Clause 4** — Context of the organization - **Clause 5** — Leadership & Energy Policy - **Clause 6** — Planning (energy review, EnPIs, baselines, objectives, action plans) - **Clause 7** — Support (competence, awareness, communication, documented information) - **Clause 8** — Operation (operational controls, design, procurement) - **Clause 9** — Performance evaluation (monitoring, measurement, analysis, internal audit, management review) - **Clause 10** — Improvement (nonconformity, continual improvement) ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 The Plan-Do-Check-Act DNA of ISO 50001 Every EnMS clause sits inside the **PDCA wheel**. This is the *core mental model* a KTU examiner expects you to draw and explain. #### **PLAN** (Clauses 4, 5, 6) - Understand the **organizational context** — internal issues (ageing boilers, tariff structure) and external issues (PAT scheme, carbon tax, export energy norms). - Define the **scope and boundaries** of the EnMS — e.g., "EnMS covers the milk reception to finished-goods warehouse of Plant A." - Write the **Energy Policy** — a top-management-signed commitment. - Conduct the **Initial Energy Review (IER)**. - Establish the **Energy Baseline (EnB)** and **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)**. - Set **Energy Objectives** and **Energy Targets** with **Action Plans**. #### **DO** (Clauses 7, 8) - Provide **resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information**. - Implement **operational controls** — SOPs for boilers, chillers, compressed air, lighting, steam traps. - Control **design** of new installations and **energy procurement**. #### **CHECK** (Clause 9) - **Monitor, measure, and analyze** energy performance. - Conduct **internal audits** of the EnMS. - Perform **management review** at planned intervals. #### **ACT** (Clause 10) - Identify **nonconformities** and take **corrective actions**. - Drive **continual improvement** of energy performance. --- ### 2.2 KTU Cheat-Sheet — Mandatory EnMS Terms (Learn the Definitions Cold) | # | Term | Definition (KTU-exam-ready) | Typical Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **EnB (Energy Baseline)** | Quantitative reference point that quantifies energy performance over a defined time period. | kWh/ton, MJ/kg | | 2 | **EnPI (Energy Performance Indicator)** | A measurable value (ratio or absolute) used to track EnMS performance. | kWh/ton product | | 3 | **SEC (Specific Energy Consumption)** | Energy consumed per unit of output (same as EnPI in many plants). | MJ/kg, kWh/ton | | 4 | **M&V (Measurement & Verification)** | Process to confirm that energy savings are real, persistent, and as projected. | Per IPMVP / ISO 50015 | | 5 | **SEU (Significant Energy Use)** | Energy use accounting for substantial consumption and offering savings potential. | % of total energy | | 6 | **Energy Review** | Determination of current energy performance, SEUs, and improvement opportunities. | Documented report | | 7 | **Action Plan** | Documented plan to achieve energy objectives and targets. | Targets + responsibilities + dates | | 8 | **Energy Policy** | Top-management statement of commitment to energy performance. | 1-page signed document | | 9 | **Operational Control** | Documented procedure ensuring SEUs are performed efficiently. | SOPs, log sheets | | 10 | **Management Review** | Formal review of EnMS by top management at planned intervals. | Minutes of meeting | | 11 | **Continual Improvement** | Recurring process of enhancing the EnMS to achieve sustained improvement. | Year-on-year kWh/ton reduction | | 12 | **Energy Team** | Cross-functional group with defined roles, responsibilities, and authority. | Org chart + RACI | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Trick:** The difference between **EnB** and **EnPI** is a *favourite 3-mark question*. > - **EnB = Past** (the reference "fingerprint" of how the plant used to perform) > - **EnPI = Present/Future** (the live KPI measured against the EnB) --- ### 2.3 The Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) Equation — Your Master Formula The single most important quantitative metric in EnMS is the **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**: $$ \text{SEC} = \frac{\text{Total Energy Consumed in a Period}}{\text{Total Production Output in Same Period}} $$ In SI practice, the total energy is converted into a single common unit — **MJ (Mega-Joule)** or **GJ (Giga-Joule)** — using standard calorific/electrical conversion factors: | Energy Source | Conversion Factor | |---|---| | 1 kWh of electricity | **3.6 MJ** | | 1 kg of furnace oil | **40.0 MJ** (Gross Calorific Value) | | 1 kg of natural gas | **52.0 MJ** (GCV) | | 1 kg of LPG | **46.0 MJ** (GCV) | | 1 kg of coal (grade B–C) | **18.0–24.0 MJ** | | 1 ton of refrigeration-hour (TR-hr) | **12.66 MJ** | | 1 Nm³ of compressed air @ 7 bar | **~0.28 MJ** (electrical equivalent) | The **Energy Performance Improvement (EPI)** percentage is then: $$ \text{EPI (\%)} = \frac{\text{SEC}_{\text{baseline}} - \text{SEC}_{\text{current}}}{\text{SEC}_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100 $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the formula a KTU examiner will use to grade any "calculation of energy savings" sub-question in Part B. --- ### 2.4 Why Food Processing Demands a Sector-Specific EnMS A food plant is **unique** because: 1. **Hygiene-energy paradox** — strict food-safety (FSSAI, HACCP) drives higher cleaning, cold-chain, and sterilization energy use, so *every* kWh saved is a direct margin gain. 2. **Load variability** — seasonal raw material arrivals (sugarcane crushing in 4 months, milk flush in winter) demand *dynamic* baselines. 3. **Co-generation opportunity** — bagasse in sugar, biogas from ETP in distilleries, rice-husk in rice mills means *fuel substitution* is a primary lever. 4. **Process-stream integration** — pinch analysis (heat recovery between pasteurizer outlet and CIP inlet) gives 10–30% steam savings. 5. **Refrigeration load is huge** — dairy, meat, marine, and ice-cream plants spend 40–60% of electrical energy on refrigeration. Hence a *generic* ISO 50001 template must be **customised** to capture the **boiler house, steam line, chiller plant, compressor house, dryer, and evaporator** as discrete SEUs. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation Procedure (Analytical Matrix) The KTU 2024 Scheme expects a student to be able to *write down* the implementation steps in the correct order. Below is the **exhaustive 11-step procedure** used by BEE-empanelled EnMS consultants in Indian food plants. --- ### **STEP 1 — Top Management Commitment & Energy Policy** - Top management issues a **signed Energy Policy** declaring commitment to energy efficiency, legal compliance, and continual improvement. - The policy is communicated to all employees, contractors, and visitors. - A **Management Representative (MR)** — often the Plant Head or a senior engineer — is appointed. - An **Energy Manager** is designated (mandatory for Designated Consumers under PAT Scheme of BEE). > **Why:** Without a top-down mandate, no shop-floor change is sustainable. ISO 50001 *requires* Clause 5 leadership evidence. --- ### **STEP 2 — Define Scope, Boundaries, and Organizational Context** - **Scope statement:** e.g., "This EnMS covers the receipt of raw milk, processing, pasteurization, packaging, cold storage, and dispatch at Plant A, Kerala." - **Boundaries:** physical (perimeter wall), organizational (legal entity), and activity-based (milk processing only — not the attached cattle farm). - **Context analysis (Clause 4):** - *Internal issues:* ageing ammonia compressor, variable milk throughput, low boiler efficiency. - *External issues:* Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) tariff revision, FSSAI energy norms, carbon-credit market. --- ### **STEP 3 — Form the Energy Team** - Cross-functional team: Maintenance, Production, Quality, Purchase, Finance, Utilities. - **RACI matrix** is drawn — *Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed*. - Team members are trained on ISO 50001, energy auditing, and M&V. --- ### **STEP 4 — Conduct the Initial Energy Review (IER)** The IER is the **single most important deliverable**. It has 4 sub-steps: #### 4a. Energy Consumption Data Collection - Collect **last 24–36 months** of utility bills (electricity, HSD, furnace oil, LPG, coal, biomass). - Tabulate **monthly kWh, tonnes of steam, kg of fuel**. - Plot **load curves** from HT meter data (15-minute interval data if available). #### 4b. Energy Mapping - Draw a **Single-Line Energy Flow Diagram** from utility intake to end-use: ``` Grid → Transformer → LT Panel → HT Motors (Compressors, Pumps, Mills) → LT Motors (Conveyors, Fans, Agitators) → Lighting → HVAC Fuel Yard → Boiler → Steam Header → Process (Pasteurizer, Evaporator, CIP) → Space heating DG Set → Emergency Loads ``` - Quantify energy at **each node** with calibrated meters. #### 4c. Identify Significant Energy Uses (SEUs) - **Pareto analysis** — typically 20% of equipment consumes 80% of energy. - For a food plant, SEUs almost always include: - **Boiler** (steam generation) - **Chiller / Ammonia Compressor** (refrigeration) - **Compressed Air System** - **Evaporator / Dryer** - **Effluent Treatment Pump House** - **Lighting** (in large warehouses) #### 4d. Identify Improvement Opportunities - For each SEU, list energy-saving opportunities (ESOs) with: - Estimated annual energy savings - Estimated capital cost - Simple Payback Period (SPP) - CO₂ reduction $$ \text{SPP (years)} = \frac{\text{Capital Investment (₹)}}{\text{Annual Monetary Savings (₹/year)}} $$ > **Why:** The IER is the *single source of truth* that justifies all later EnMS investment decisions. --- ### **STEP 5 — Establish Energy Baseline (EnB)** - **Fix the reference period** — e.g., "April 2022 – March 2023, FY 2022-23." - **Fix the reference data** — total energy in MJ, total production in tonnes, weather-normalised if relevant. - **Normalize EnB** for variables that affect energy but are not under plant control — e.g., ambient temperature for refrigeration, cane quality for sugar. - The EnB is **frozen** unless a major change occurs (capacity expansion, new process). Any change triggers a **re-baselining protocol**. > **Example EnB for a dairy plant:** > EnB = **180 kWh per ton of milk processed** (FY 2022-23, normalized to 28°C ambient). --- ### **STEP 6 — Define Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)** - Each SEU must have at least one EnPI. - EnPIs can be: - **Ratio form (most common):** kWh/ton, kg-steam/ton, MJ/kg. - **Absolute form:** Total kWh, total tonnes of CO₂. - **Statistical form:** regression-model-based EnPI = a·X₁ + b·X₂ + c, where X₁, X₂ are production variables. | SEU | Recommended EnPI | |---|---| | Boiler | kg of steam per ton of product (or boiler efficiency %) | | Chiller | kWh/TR of refrigeration output (or COP) | | Compressed Air | Nm³/kWh or specific power (kW per 100 Nm³/min) | | Dryer | MJ of fuel per kg of water evaporated | | Lighting | kWh/m²-month | --- ### **STEP 7 — Set Energy Objectives, Targets, and Action Plans** - **Objective (qualitative):** "Reduce steam consumption in the dairy plant by 15% in 24 months." - **Target (quantitative):** "Bring steam SEC from 280 kg/ton to 238 kg/ton by March 2025." - **Action Plan (5W1H):** - **What:** Install VFD on boiler FD/ID fans + condensate recovery. - **Who:** Maintenance Head (R), Energy Manager (A). - **When:** By Dec 2024. - **Where:** Boiler house, Plant A. - **Why:** 15% steam reduction target. - **How:** Capex ₹45 lakh, expected savings ₹18 lakh/year, SPP 2.5 years. --- ### **STEP 8 — Implement Operational Controls** Documented **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** are written for every SEU: | SEU | Sample Operational Control | |---|---| | Boiler | SOP for burner tuning, blowdown schedule, O₂ trim, TDS limit | | Chiller | SOP for condenser cleaning, set-point logic, defrost cycle | | Compressed Air | SOP for leak survey, pressure set-point, drain traps | | Steam | SOP for trap inspection, insulation, condensate flashing | **Permit-to-work, lock-out-tag-out (LOTO), and maintenance schedules** are integrated. --- ### **STEP 9 — Training, Competence, and Awareness** - Every employee working on an SEU is **trained and certified**. - **Awareness campaigns:** poster displays, monthly tool-box talks, energy dashboards on shop floor. - **Energy Champions** are identified on each shift. - Records of **competence, training, and awareness** are maintained (Clause 7.2). --- ### **STEP 10 — Monitoring, Measurement, and Analysis (M&V)** - **Calibrated meters** are installed at every SEU boundary. - M&V is done per **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)** or **ISO 50015**. - **Energy data is collected daily**, summarised weekly, and reviewed monthly. - **Variances > 5%** trigger a root-cause analysis (5-Why / Ishikawa). $$ \text{Variance \%} = \frac{\text{Actual EnPI} - \text{Planned EnPI}}{\text{Planned EnPI}} \times 100 $$ --- ### **STEP 11 — Internal Audit, Management Review, and Continual Improvement** - **Internal audits** are conducted every 6–12 months by trained internal auditors (Clause 9.2). - **Nonconformities** are documented with corrective and preventive actions (Clause 10.2). - **Management Review (Clause 9.3)** — top management meets at least annually to review: - Results of internal audits - EnPI performance vs targets - Status of action plans - Changing legal/regulatory context - Recommendations for improvement - The cycle restarts — *Continual Improvement (Clause 10.3)*. --- ### 3.1 Summary Table — The 11 Steps at a Glance | Step | Clause | Output Document | Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 5.1, 5.2 | Energy Policy | Once, revised as needed | | 2 | 4.1, 4.3 | Scope Statement | Once | | 3 | 5.3, 7.1 | Energy Team Charter | Once | | 4 | 6.3 | Initial Energy Review Report | Once, then updated | | 5 | 6.4 | Energy Baseline (EnB) | Once, re-baselined on change | | 6 | 6.4 | EnPI Register | Once, updated as needed | | 7 | 6.5 | Objectives, Targets, Action Plans | Annual | | 8 | 8.1, 8.2 | SOPs, Operational Controls | Continuous | | 9 | 7.2, 7.3 | Training Records | Continuous | | 10 | 9.1, 9.4 | Energy M&V Reports | Daily/Weekly/Monthly | | 11 | 9.2, 9.3, 10 | Audit Reports, MR Minutes, CAPA Log | 6–12 monthly | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The PDCA Cycle Mapped to ISO 50001 Clauses ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef plan fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000 classDef do fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000 classDef check fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207,color:#000 classDef act fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000 PStart(["START of EnMS"]) --> P1["Clause 4: Context of Organization
