[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Differentiate between Energy Baseline (EnB) and Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI).** **Model Answer:** - **Energy Baseline (EnB):** A *quantitative reference* that documents the energy performance of an organization over a defined historical time period, used as a comparison point for measuring improvement. Example: *190 kWh/ton of milk processed in FY 2022-23.* - **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI):** A *measurable value* (ratio or absolute) used by the organization to track and compare energy performance improvement. Example: *monthly kWh/ton of milk processed measured against the EnB.* - The **EnB is fixed** (unless re-baselined); the **EnPI is dynamic** and tracked continuously. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse EnB with EnPI or treat them as interchangeable. EnB = *past reference fingerprint*; EnPI = *live KPI*. Loss of 1 mark is common here. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **List any six clauses of ISO 50001:2018 and their purpose in a food processing EnMS.** **Model Answer (6 of the 10 clauses):** 1. **Clause 4 – Context of Organization:** Defines the scope, boundaries, internal/external issues. 2. **Clause 5 – Leadership:** Top management commitment, Energy Policy. 3. **Clause 6 – Planning:** Initial Energy Review, EnB, EnPIs, objectives, action plans. 4. **Clause 7 – Support:** Competence, awareness, communication, documented information. 5. **Clause 8 – Operation:** Operational controls, design, procurement. 6. **Clause 9 – Performance Evaluation:** Monitoring & measurement, internal audit, management review. --- ### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Initial Energy Review (IER)** as per ISO 50001, listing its four major outputs and explaining how a Pareto chart is used to identify Significant Energy Uses (SEUs) in a food processing plant. **(b) [7 Marks]** A dairy plant in Kerala processes **120 tonnes of milk per day**, working **300 days/year**. The plant consumes: - Electricity: **18,000 kWh/day** at ₹9.50/kWh - Furnace Oil: **1,800 kg/day** at ₹85/kg (GCV = 40 MJ/kg) - Lignite Coal: **2,400 kg/day** at ₹12/kg (GCV = 18 MJ/kg) **Calculate:** (i) Total annual energy consumption in **GJ**. (ii) Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in **MJ/kg of milk processed**. (iii) Annual energy cost in **₹ Lakhs**. (iv) If a VFD retrofit saves **8%** electrical energy, calculate the **annual monetary saving** in ₹ and the **CO₂ avoided** (assume 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh grid emission factor). --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question Bank]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle** as applied to the implementation of an EnMS in a food processing unit. Map each phase to the relevant ISO 50001 clauses and give at least **two examples of operational controls** for a steam-based pasteurizer SEU. **(b) [7 Marks]** A sugar mill in UP crushes **5,000 TCD (tonnes of cane per day)** for **150 days** per season. It uses **bagasse** as boiler fuel and **grid electricity** for milling. - Grid electricity: **22 kWh/ton of cane** at ₹8.00/kWh - Bagasse generated: **0.30 tonnes/ton of cane** with GCV **7,500 kJ/kg** used 100% in boiler. - A **condensate recovery** project recovers **6 kg of flash steam per ton of cane** (latent heat of steam = 2,257 kJ/kg). If the project costs **₹60 lakh** and bagasse is valued at **₹4,000/tonne**, calculate: (i) Annual flash-steam energy recovered in **GJ**. (ii) Annual monetary value of bagasse saved in **₹ Lakhs**. (iii) **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** in years. --- ### 5.3 Model Solutions --- #### **Solution to Question A:** **(a) Initial Energy Review (IER) [7 Marks]:** The IER is the **first technical deliverable** of an EnMS (ISO 50001 Clause 6.3). It has four major outputs: 1. **Energy Consumption Profile** — past 24–36 months of energy use by source, plotted monthly. 2. **Energy Flow Map (Single-Line Diagram)** — from utility intake to end-use, with energy quantified at each node. 3. **List of Significant Energy Uses (SEUs)** — identified by **Pareto analysis**. 4. **Energy-Saving Opportunities (ESOs)** — each with savings estimate, cost, and payback. **Pareto Method for SEU Identification:** - List all energy-consuming equipment with their **annual energy (GJ)** and **% of total**. - Sort in **descending order**. - Compute **cumulative %**. - Equipment crossing the **80% cumulative line** are **SEUs**. *Example for a dairy plant:* - Boiler (Steam): 45% - Chiller: 22% - Compressed Air: 8% - Lighting: 6% - Pumps & Motors: 12% - HVAC: 4% - Others: 3% - **Cumulative top-3 = 75%, top-4 = 81% → First 4 are SEUs.** **(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:** **Given:** 120 TPD milk, 300 days/year. **(i) Annual energy in GJ:** Electrical: 18,000 kWh/day × 300 = **54,00,000 kWh/year** $$ E_{\text{elec}} = 54,00,000 \times 3.6 = 1,94,40,000 \text{ MJ} = 19,440 \text{ GJ} $$ FO: 1,800 × 300 = 5,40,000 kg $$ E_{\text{FO}} = 5,40,000 \times 40 = 2,16,00,000 \text{ MJ} = 21,600 \text{ GJ} $$ Coal: 2,400 × 300 = 7,20,000 kg $$ E_{\text{coal}} = 7,20,000 \times 18 = 1,29,60,000 \text{ MJ} = 12,960 \text{ GJ} $$ $$ E_{\text{total}} = 19,440 + 21,600 + 12,960 = \mathbf{54,000 \text{ GJ/year}} $$ **(ii) SEC in MJ/kg milk:** Annual milk = 120 × 300 × 1000 = **3,60,00,000 kg** $$ \text{SEC} = \frac{54,00,000 \times 1000 \text{ MJ}}{3,60,00,000 \text{ kg}} = \mathbf{15.0 \text{ MJ/kg}} $$ **(iii) Annual energy cost:** $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{elec}} = 54,00,000 \times 9.50 = ₹5,13,00,000 $$ $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{FO}} = 5,40,000 \times 85 = ₹4,59,00,000 $$ $$ \text{Cost}_{\text{coal}} = 7,20,000 \times 12 = ₹86,40,000 $$ $$ \text{Total Cost} = 5,13,00,000 + 4,59,00,000 + 86,40,000 = ₹10,58,40,000 = \mathbf{₹105.84 \text{ Lakhs}} $$ **(iv) VFD savings:** Saving = 8% of 54,00,000 = **4,32,000 kWh/year** $$ \text{Money saved} = 4,32,000 \times 9.50 = \mathbf{₹41,04,000} \;(\text{₹41.04 Lakhs}) $$ $$ \text{CO}_2 \text{ avoided} = 4,32,000 \times 0.82 = \mathbf{3,54,240 \text{ kg}} = \mathbf{354.24 \text{ tonnes CO}_2 $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Common errors: forgetting to convert **kWh to MJ** (multiply by 3.6); using 1000 kg = 1 tonne correctly; not multiplying daily × days. Always write units in *every line*. Marks are deducted for missing units. --- #### **Solution to Question B:** **(a) PDCA Cycle & Operational Controls [7 Marks]:** | PDCA Phase | ISO 50001 Clause | Action in a Food Plant | |---|---|---| | **PLAN** | 4, 5, 6 | Policy, scope, IER, EnB, EnPIs, action plan | | **DO** | 7, 8 | SOPs, training, operational controls, procurement | | **CHECK** | 9 | M&V, internal audit, management review | | **ACT** | 10 | Corrective action, continual improvement | **Two operational controls for steam-based pasteurizer SEU:** 1. **SOP for Boiler Tuning:** Daily O₂ trim, weekly soot-blowing, monthly burner calibration, blowdown at TDS < 3500 ppm. 2. **SOP for Steam Line:** Weekly steam-trap inspection (ultrasonic), insulation audit, condensate recovery, flash-steam utilisation. **(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:** **Given:** 5,000 TCD × 150 days = 7,50,000 tonnes cane/season. **(i) Annual flash-steam energy recovered:** Flash steam = 6 kg/ton × 7,50,000 = **45,00,000 kg** = 4,500 tonnes $$ E_{\text{flash}} = 45,00,000 \times 2,257 \text{ kJ} = 1,01,56,50,00,000 \text{ kJ} $$ $$ = 1,01,565 \text{ GJ} = \mathbf{1,01,565 \text{ GJ/season}} $$ **(ii) Monetary value of bagasse saved:** Energy equivalent of bagasse = 7,500 kJ/kg, so 1 tonne bagasse = 7.5 GJ $$ \text{Bagasse saved} = \frac{1,01,565 \text{ GJ}}{7.5 \text{ GJ/tonne}} = 13,542 \text{ tonnes} $$ $$ \text{Value} = 13,542 \times 4,000 = ₹5,41,68,000 = \mathbf{₹54.17 \text{ Lakhs}} $$ **(iii) Simple Payback Period:** $$ \text{SPP} = \frac{₹60,00,000}{₹54,17,000/\text{year}} = \mathbf{1.11 \text{ years}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to convert flash-steam energy to bagasse equivalent using the GCV. Use **GCV = 7,500 kJ/kg for bagasse**. Also note the SPP formula requires *capital investment* in numerator and *annual savings* in denominator — *not* the other way. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** - **EnMS =** ISO 50001 framework, NOT a software. - **10 clauses (4–10)** built on the **PDCA cycle** and **High-Level Structure (HLS)**. - **Energy Policy** must be top-management-signed (Clause 5.2). - **Initial Energy Review (IER)** = 4 outputs: profile, flow map, SEUs, ESOs. - **Pareto 80/20 rule** identifies **SEUs**. - **EnB (fixed, historical) ≠ EnPI (live, current)** — the #1 favourite differentiator question. - **SEC formula** = Total Energy / Total Output; convert *all* energy to MJ/GJ first. - **Conversion factors to memorise:** 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ; FO = 40 MJ/kg; Bagasse = 7.5 MJ/kg; 1 TR-hr = 12.66 MJ. - **Action Plan** uses 5W1H format: What, Who, When, Where, Why, How. - **Operational controls** are **SOPs** for every SEU (boiler, chiller, compressor, steam line). - **M&V** must follow **IPMVP** or **ISO 50015**. - **Internal audit every 6–12 months**, **Management Review at least annually**. - **Continual improvement** is the *spiral* not a one-time fix. - **Food plant SEUs** are *almost always*: Boiler, Chiller, Compressed Air, Evaporator, Dryer, ETP, Lighting. - **SPP** = Capital Investment / Annual Savings (years). - **CO₂ emission factor** for Indian grid ≈ **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** (CEA 2022 baseline). - **Energy Manager** is **mandatory** for Designated Consumers under the **PAT Scheme** of BEE. > **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** A 14-mark answer always scores higher if you (1) **draw a block diagram**, (2) **tabulate** clauses vs actions, and (3) **end with a numerical example** — even if not asked, it shows depth and is rewarded under OBE evaluation.

topics.title: Monitoring, Measurement, and Verification Techniques for EnMS Effectiveness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INSUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Monitoring, Measurement, and Verification Techniques for EnMS Effectiveness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monitoring, Measurement, and Verification (MMV)** refers to the systematic and planned process of collecting, analysing, and converting energy-related data into actionable information that quantifies the actual energy performance of a facility, process, or organisation against an established baseline. Under **ISO 50001:2018**, MMV is the operational backbone of Clause 9.1 (Monitoring, Measurement, Analysis and Evaluation), Clause 9.1.1 (General), and Clause 9.1.2 (Evaluation of Compliance).

**Operational Definition:** MMV is the closed-loop data-to-decision pipeline that confirms whether the Energy Management System (EnMS) is producing the planned energy savings, while simultaneously detecting deviations, anomalies, and opportunities for continual improvement.

The three pillars of MMV in the ISO 50001 framework are:

| Pillar | KTU Standard Reference | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Monitoring** | Clause 9.1 | Continuous observation and recording of energy flows |
| **Measurement** | Clause 9.1 | Quantitative determination of energy and energy-related variables using calibrated instruments |
| **Verification** | Clause 9.1 / 9.2 | Confirming accuracy, reliability, and effectiveness of the EnMS outcomes |

> [!NOTE]
> **EnMS** = Energy Management System. An EnMS is the complete set of policies, processes, procedures, and records that an organisation uses to manage its energy performance, formally structured under ISO 50001.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Calorie Tracker" of an Industry

Imagine you are trying to lose weight. You do not simply "eat less" — you weigh yourself daily (monitor), measure the calories of every meal (measure), and at the end of the month you compare your weight against your starting weight to see if the diet worked (verify). Without this loop, you are guessing.

An industrial EnMS works identically. The factory cannot simply "use less energy" — it must install meters (monitor), log energy use in kWh, MJ, or GJ (measure), and compare actual use against a baseline to verify savings. If the baseline (starting weight) was 1000 kWh/day and current use is 850 kWh/day, the **verified savings = 150 kWh/day** (15% improvement). This verification loop is what gives management, regulators, and auditors the confidence that the EnMS is actually working.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics Used in EnMS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard energy metrics that must be mastered for the KTU board exam:
> - **kWh** — Kilowatt-hour (electrical energy)
> - **MJ / GJ** — MegaJoule / GigaJoule (thermal energy, useful for steam, fuel)
> - **TOE** — Tonne of Oil Equivalent (1 TOE ≈ 41.868 GJ)
> - **kWh/m², kWh/tonne, kWh/kg** — Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)
> - **EnPI** — Energy Performance Indicator
> - **EnB** — Energy Baseline

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Sequential Phases of MMV in EnMS

The MMV process within an EnMS follows a rigorous four-phase lifecycle. Each phase is mandatory under ISO 50001 Clause 9.1.

### Phase 1 — Planning the MMV Framework
Before any meter is installed, the organisation must define:
- **Scope** of the EnMS (single line, entire plant, multi-site)
- **Boundaries** (organisational, operational, physical)
- **Significant Energy Uses (SEUs)** identified during the energy review
- **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)** that will track progress
- **Energy Baseline (EnB)** as the reference point
- **Measurement Plan** detailing what to measure, how, when, and with what accuracy

### Phase 2 — Data Acquisition (Monitoring & Measurement)
This phase involves:
- **Instrumentation**: Installation of calibrated meters (electrical, thermal, flow, gas)
- **Data Logging**: Continuous (real-time SCADA) or periodic (manual) recording
- **Data Validation**: Cross-checking sensor readings for plausibility (e.g., negative values, out-of-range spikes)
- **Calibration Schedule**: Ensuring measurement uncertainty stays within ±2% typically

### Phase 3 — Analysis and Verification
- Comparison of current EnPI values against the EnB
- **CUSUM (Cumulative Sum)** statistical analysis to detect drift
- **Regression-based normalisation** to adjust EnB for changes in production volume, weather, or feedstock
- Calculation of **Energy Performance Improvement (EnPI %)**
- Reporting deviations to top management (ISO 50001 Clause 9.1.1)

### Phase 4 — Corrective Action and Continual Improvement
- Root-cause analysis of any shortfall in EnPI
- Update of the **Energy Review** if new SEUs emerge
- Re-baselining only when justified (e.g., major process change, capacity expansion)
- Communication of verified results to all stakeholders

## 2.2 Energy Baseline (EnB) — The Reference Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Energy Baseline (EnB)** is the quantitative reference that provides a basis for comparison of energy performance. It is established using historical data, normalised for variables that significantly affect energy use.

Mathematical representation:

$$
E_{baseline} = f(x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots, x_n)
$$

where each $x_i$ is a variable that significantly influences energy consumption (production output, ambient temperature, humidity, feedstock moisture content).

A simple linear EnB model commonly used in food industry (e.g., dairy, bakery, beverage):

$$
E_{baseline} = a_0 + a_1 \cdot P + a_2 \cdot T
$$

- $E_{baseline}$ = baseline energy consumption (kWh or GJ)
- $P$ = production output (tonnes or kg)
- $T$ = ambient temperature (°C)
- $a_0, a_1, a_2$ = regression coefficients derived from historical data

## 2.3 Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)

> [!NOTE]
> An **EnPI** is a metric or unit of measure that management uses to track and assess the energy performance of an organisation. Common EnPIs in the food industry include:
> - **kWh per tonne of finished product** (most common)
> - **MJ per kg of milk processed**
> - **Specific Steam Consumption (SSC)** in kg steam per kg product
> - **COP** (Coefficient of Performance) of refrigeration systems
> - **kWh per m³ of cold storage**

The EnPI value is calculated as:

$$
\text{EnPI} = \frac{\text{Total Energy Consumption in Period}}{\text{Production Output in Period}}
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Energy Consumption | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{E}{Q}$ | kWh/tonne | Tracking energy intensity |
| 2 | Energy Performance Improvement (%) | $\% \text{EnPI} = \dfrac{\text{EnPI}_{baseline} - \text{EnPI}_{current}}{\text{EnPI}_{baseline}} \times 100$ | % | Year-on-year improvement |
| 3 | Absolute Energy Savings | $\Delta E = E_{baseline,adjusted} - E_{actual}$ | kWh, GJ, TOE | Total energy avoided |
| 4 | Linear Energy Baseline | $E_b = a_0 + a_1 P + a_2 T$ | kWh | Regression-based normalisation |
| 5 | Coefficient of Performance | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{electric}}$ | dimensionless | Refrigeration efficiency |
| 6 | Boiler Efficiency | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_s (h_s - h_{fw})}{m_f \cdot \text{GCV}_f} \times 100$ | % | Steam system performance |
| 7 | CUSUM Statistic | $S_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t}(X_i - \mu_0)$ | kWh | Drift detection in EnPI |
| 8 | TOE Conversion | $1 \text{ TOE} = 41.868 \text{ GJ}$ | TOE ↔ GJ | Cross-fuel comparison |
| 9 | Measurement Uncertainty | $u_c = \sqrt{\sum u_i^2}$ | % | Combined uncertainty per ISO/IEC Guide |
| 10 | M&V Option A (Retrofit Isolation) | Retrofit Isolation, key parameter measurement | kW or kWh | IPMVP Option A |
| 11 | M&V Option B (Retrofit Isolation) | All parameter measurement | kWh | IPMVP Option B |
| 12 | M&V Option C (Whole Facility) | Whole-facility M&V using EnB | kWh | IPMVP Option C |
| 13 | M&V Option D (Calibrated Simulation) | Calibrated simulation | kWh | IPMVP Option D |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IPMVP** = International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol. It defines four standardised M&V Options (A, B, C, D). KTU board exam frequently asks students to identify which M&V option suits which scenario — pay close attention!

## 2.5 IPMVP M&V Options — The Four-Option Framework

> [!NOTE]
> The **IPMVP** (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol) is the global standard for M&V. KTU examiners expect students to know all four options.

| Option | Name | What is Measured | Accuracy | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Retrofit Isolation — Key Parameter | One key parameter (e.g., kW) + stipulated others | ±20% | Lighting retrofits, VFDs |
| **B** | Retrofit Isolation — All Parameters | All energy parameters | ±10% | Compressed air systems, boilers |
| **C** | Whole Facility | Whole-facility energy using EnB | ±10% | Multi-measure projects, lighting + HVAC + process |
| **D** | Calibrated Simulation | Calibrated computer simulation | ±5% (model dependent) | New construction, complex retrofits |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a typical Kerala food processing plant (e.g., a rice mill or dairy):
- A **boiler** retrofit (replacing coal-fired with biomass-fired) would typically use **IPMVP Option B** — measure steam output (kg/hr) and fuel consumption (kg/hr) directly.
- A **refrigeration plant** upgrade would typically use **IPMVP Option A** — measure compressor kW and use manufacturer's stipulated COP curves.
- A **complete plant energy audit** with multiple interventions (lighting + motor + insulation + refrigeration) would use **IPMVP Option C** — whole facility M&V against the Energy Baseline.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Performance Improvement Percentage

**Given:**
- Baseline period energy consumption: $E_b$ (kWh)
- Baseline period production: $Q_b$ (tonnes)
- Current period energy consumption: $E_c$ (kWh)
- Current period production: $Q_c$ (tonnes)

**Find:** Energy Performance Improvement percentage (%EnPI)

### Step 1 — Compute Baseline Specific Energy Consumption

$$
\text{SEC}_b = \frac{E_b}{Q_b}
$$

**Logic:** This normalises the baseline energy use against the amount of product manufactured. Without normalisation, a year with low production would falsely appear "energy efficient."

### Step 2 — Compute Current Specific Energy Consumption

$$
\text{SEC}_c = \frac{E_c}{Q_c}
$$

**Logic:** Same normalisation applied to the current period. Both SEC values are now directly comparable because they are per unit of output.

### Step 3 — Compute Performance Improvement

$$
\% \text{EnPI} = \frac{\text{SEC}_b - \text{SEC}_c}{\text{SEC}_b} \times 100
$$

**Logic:** If SEC has gone down, the numerator is positive — indicating improvement. Divided by the baseline, scaled to percentage. This is the headline metric reported to management.

### Step 4 — Compute Absolute Energy Savings (optional but KTU-favoured)

$$
\Delta E_{absolute} = E_b - E_c
$$

**Logic:** The total energy avoided in raw units. Useful for cost calculation when multiplied by the unit energy cost.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Food Industry Boiler Retrofit

**Scenario:** A spice processing plant in Kerala replaces its old coal-fired boiler with a new biomass-fired boiler. Using IPMVP Option B, the following data is collected:

| Parameter | Baseline (Coal) | Post-Retrofit (Biomass) |
|---|---|---|
| Annual steam production $m_s$ | 5,000 tonnes/yr | 5,000 tonnes/yr |
| Specific enthalpy of steam $h_s$ | 2,800 kJ/kg | 2,800 kJ/kg |
| Feedwater enthalpy $h_{fw}$ | 420 kJ/kg | 420 kJ/kg |
| Fuel consumption $m_f$ | 800 tonnes/yr | 1,100 tonnes/yr |
| GCV of fuel | 25,000 kJ/kg (coal) | 17,000 kJ/kg (biomass) |

**Compute the energy savings in GJ and the % improvement in boiler efficiency.**

### Step 1 — Compute Baseline Boiler Efficiency

$$
\eta_{boiler,base} = \frac{m_s (h_s - h_{fw})}{m_f \cdot GCV_f} \times 100
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\eta_{boiler,base} = \frac{5{,}000{,}000 \text{ kg} \times (2800 - 420) \text{ kJ/kg}}{800{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 25{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{boiler,base} = \frac{5{,}000{,}000 \times 2380}{800{,}000 \times 25{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{boiler,base} = \frac{1.19 \times 10^{10}}{2.0 \times 10^{10}} \times 100 = 59.5\%
$$

**Logic:** Only 59.5% of the coal's energy content is converted into useful steam heat. The rest is lost as flue gas, radiation, and incomplete combustion.

### Step 2 — Compute Post-Retrofit Boiler Efficiency

$$
\eta_{boiler,post} = \frac{5{,}000{,}000 \times 2380}{1{,}100{,}000 \times 17{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{boiler,post} = \frac{1.19 \times 10^{10}}{1.87 \times 10^{10}} \times 100 = 63.6\%
$$

**Logic:** Efficiency has improved from 59.5% to 63.6%, an increase of 4.1 percentage points.

### Step 3 — Compute Energy Savings in GJ

Total energy input baseline:

$$
E_{input,base} = m_f \cdot GCV_f = 800{,}000 \times 25{,}000 = 2.0 \times 10^{10} \text{ kJ} = 20{,}000 \text{ GJ}
$$

Total energy input post-retrofit:

$$
E_{input,post} = 1{,}100{,}000 \times 17{,}000 = 1.87 \times 10^{10} \text{ kJ} = 18{,}700 \text{ GJ}
$$

Absolute energy input saving:

$$
\Delta E_{input} = 20{,}000 - 18{,}700 = 1{,}300 \text{ GJ/yr}
$$

Converting to TOE:

$$
\Delta E_{TOE} = \frac{1{,}300}{41.868} \approx 31.05 \text{ TOE/yr}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute % Improvement in Boiler Efficiency

$$
\% \text{Improvement} = \frac{63.6 - 59.5}{59.5} \times 100 = 6.89\%
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right M&V Option

| Project Type | Recommended IPMVP Option | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| LED lighting replacement in a bakery | **Option A** | Single key parameter (kW lighting load); savings can be calculated from lighting kW and operating hours. Stipulated values used for non-key parameters. |
| Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) on a feed mill motor | **Option A** | Measure motor kW before and after; use manufacturer's pump/fan affinity laws for stipulated values. |
| Biomass boiler in a rice mill | **Option B** | Measure all parameters: steam mass flow, feedwater temp, fuel mass flow, GCV. High accuracy required. |
| Complete plant energy retrofit (multiple interventions) | **Option C** | Whole-facility M&V using established EnB; regression-based normalisation for production, weather. |
| New greenfield dairy plant | **Option D** | Calibrated simulation using eQuest or EnergyPlus; baseline established from model, actuals compared. |
| Compressed air leak reduction in a fruit juice plant | **Option B** | Measure compressor kW, leak flow rate (m³/min), pressure — all parameters measured directly. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The MMV Closed-Loop Cycle (ISO 50001 Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["step1: Establish Energy Baseline (EnB)"] --> B["step2: Define EnPIs"]
    B --> C["step3: Install Calibrated Meters"]
    C --> D["step4: Continuous Data Logging"]
    D --> E["step5: Data Validation and Analysis"]
    E --> F{"step6: EnPI Improvement Detected?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["step7a: Document Verified Savings"]
    F -- No --> H["step7b: Root Cause Analysis"]
    H --> I["step8: Corrective Action"]
    I --> C
    G --> J["step9: Management Review"]
    I --> J
    J --> K{"step10: Major Change Detected?"}
    K -- Yes --> L["step11: Re-baseline EnB"]
    K -- No --> C
    L --> B
```

**Interpretation:** This is the continuous improvement loop required by ISO 50001 Clause 9.1 and Clause 10. Data flows forward (steps 1→9), and feedback (steps 7b, 10, 11) ensures the system self-corrects.

## 4.2 MMV Architecture in a Food Industry EnMS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["LAYER A: Energy Sources"]
        A1["stepA: Grid Electricity"]
        A2["stepB: Diesel / HSD"]
        A3["stepC: Biomass / Coal"]
        A4["stepD: LPG / PNG"]
        A5["stepE: Solar PV"]
    end

    subgraph B["LAYER B: Measurement Layer"]
        B1["stepF: Energy Meters"]
        B2["stepG: Flow Meters"]
        B3["stepH: Temp Sensors"]
        B4["stepI: Pressure Sensors"]
        B5["stepJ: Gas Analyzers"]
    end

    subgraph C["LAYER C: Data Acquisition"]
        C1["stepK: SCADA / PLC"]
        C2["stepL: Cloud EMS"]
        C3["stepM: Manual Logs"]
    end

    subgraph D["LAYER D: Analysis and Verification"]
        D1["stepN: EnB Regression"]
        D2["stepO: CUSUM Analysis"]
        D3["stepP: M&V Engine"]
        D4["stepQ: Reporting"]
    end

    subgraph E["LAYER E: Decisions"]
        E1["stepR: EnPI Dashboard"]
        E2["stepS: Alerts"]
        E3["stepT: CAPA"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B2
    A5 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C2
    B5 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D3
    C3 --> D3
    D1 --> D4
    D2 --> D4
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> E1
    D4 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
```

**Interpretation:** This layered architecture shows how energy flows from sources → through physical meters → into data acquisition systems → into the analysis and verification engine → finally into actionable dashboards and corrective actions. This is the typical architecture implemented in BRC-certified or ISO 50001-certified food plants in Kerala (e.g., Milma, Kerala Solvent, etc.).

## 4.3 The PDCA Cycle Embedded in MMV (ISO 50001:2018)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["stepP: PLAN\nEnergy Review\nDefine EnB\nSelect EnPIs"] --> D["stepD: DO\nImplement MMV\nCollect Data\nCalibrate Meters"]
    D --> C["stepC: CHECK\nAnalyse EnPIs\nCompare to EnB\nVerify Savings"]
    C --> A["stepA: ACT\nCorrective Action\nContinual Improvement\nRe-baseline if needed"]
    A --> P
```

**Interpretation:** MMV is not a one-time activity — it is the CHECK phase of the Deming/PDCA cycle embedded inside the EnMS. This is a high-frequency KTU exam diagram.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Energy Baseline (EnB). List any four factors that influence the establishment of an EnB in a food processing industry. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 mark):** An Energy Baseline (EnB) is a quantitative reference that provides a basis for comparison of energy performance over time, established using historical data and normalised for variables that significantly affect energy use.

**Factors influencing EnB establishment (2 marks — any four):**
1. Production volume / output (tonnes, kg, litres)
2. Ambient temperature and humidity (especially relevant for cold storage and dairy)
3. Raw material characteristics (e.g., moisture content of grains, fat content of milk)
4. Operating hours per day / shifts per week
5. Equipment age and maintenance condition
6. Product mix (different recipes require different energy intensities)

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between IPMVP Option A and IPMVP Option B. Give one example project where each option is most suitable. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Option A — Retrofit Isolation, Key Parameter Measurement (1 mark):** Measures one key performance parameter (e.g., kW of motor); non-key parameters are stipulated from manufacturer data or engineering estimates. Accuracy: ±20%.

> [!NOTE]
> **Option B — Retrofit Isolation, All Parameter Measurement (1 mark):** Measures all energy parameters directly using calibrated instruments. Higher accuracy: ±10%, but more costly to implement.

**Examples (1 mark):**
- **Option A example:** Replacement of incandescent lamps with LED in a bakery — measure lighting kW, stipulate operating hours.
- **Option B example:** Compressed air leak reduction in a fruit juice plant — measure compressor kW, air flow, pressure all directly.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Monitoring, Measurement and Verification of an EnMS in a Dairy Plant

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Module 3 Internal Choice Option A]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the four phases of MMV in an Energy Management System (EnMS) as per ISO 50001:2018. Discuss the role of an Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI) with two specific examples from a dairy industry. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A dairy plant consumes 8,00,000 kWh of electricity per month to process 2,000 tonnes of milk. After installing a refrigeration heat recovery system, the plant consumes 7,20,000 kWh per month to process 2,200 tonnes of milk. Calculate:
   (i) Baseline Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)
   (ii) Post-retrofit SEC
   (iii) Energy Performance Improvement percentage
   (iv) Absolute monthly energy savings
   **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution (Question A)

#### Part (a) — The Four Phases of MMV (7 Marks)

**Phase 1 — Planning the MMV Framework (2 marks):**

This phase establishes the foundation of the measurement system. Key steps include:
- Defining the **scope** of the EnMS (e.g., only refrigeration, or whole plant)
- Identifying **Significant Energy Uses (SEUs)** during the energy review — in a dairy plant, these typically include refrigeration compressors, pasteurisers, boiler, CIP (clean-in-place) systems, and packaging lines
- Selecting **EnPIs** that are measurable, controllable, and meaningful
- Establishing the **Energy Baseline (EnB)** using at least 12 months of historical data
- Preparing the **Energy Measurement Plan** documenting meters, locations, calibration frequency, data ownership, and uncertainty targets

**Phase 2 — Data Acquisition / Monitoring & Measurement (2 marks):**

This phase covers the physical installation and operation of the measurement infrastructure:
- Installation of calibrated **kWh meters** on main incomer and key sub-feeders
- Installation of **steam flow meters** on boiler outlet
- Installation of **temperature sensors** on pasteuriser inlet/outlet
- Continuous logging via SCADA or periodic manual reading (minimum monthly)
- Data validation (plausibility checks, flag outliers)
- Calibration schedule — typically annually for class 0.5 or class 1 meters

**Phase 3 — Analysis and Verification (2 marks):**

In this phase, raw data is transformed into performance information:
- Calculation of monthly EnPIs (e.g., kWh/tonne of milk processed)
- **Regression-based normalisation** of EnB using production volume, ambient temperature, and milk fat content
- **CUSUM** analysis to detect drift
- Calculation of **% EnPI improvement** month-on-month
- Verification using IPMVP protocols — for dairy refrigeration retrofit, IPMVP Option A is typically chosen
- Preparation of monthly Energy Performance Report for management

**Phase 4 — Corrective Action and Continual Improvement (1 mark):**

- Investigation of deviations from baseline
- Identification of root causes (e.g., fouled condenser, compressor wear)
- Implementation of CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action)
- Re-baselining if major changes occur (e.g., capacity expansion by >20%)
- Communication of results to all stakeholders, including annual energy report

**Two Dairy-Specific EnPI Examples (extra):**
1. **kWh per tonne of milk processed** — captures overall plant efficiency
2. **kg of steam per kg of milk powder** — captures evaporator efficiency

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Baseline electricity $E_b$ | 8,00,000 kWh/month |
| Baseline production $Q_b$ | 2,000 tonnes/month |
| Post-retrofit electricity $E_c$ | 7,20,000 kWh/month |
| Post-retrofit production $Q_c$ | 2,200 tonnes/month |

**Step 1 (1.5 marks): Baseline SEC**

$$
\text{SEC}_b = \frac{E_b}{Q_b} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{2000} = 400 \text{ kWh/tonne}
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks): Post-retrofit SEC**

$$
\text{SEC}_c = \frac{E_c}{Q_c} = \frac{7{,}20{,}000}{2200} = 327.27 \text{ kWh/tonne}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Energy Performance Improvement %**

$$
\% \text{EnPI} = \frac{\text{SEC}_b - \text{SEC}_c}{\text{SEC}_b} \times 100 = \frac{400 - 327.27}{400} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{EnPI} = \frac{72.73}{400} \times 100 = 18.18\%
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): Absolute Monthly Energy Savings**

If the plant had processed 2,200 tonnes at baseline SEC:
$$
E_{expected} = 2200 \times 400 = 8{,}80{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

Absolute saving:
$$
\Delta E = 8{,}80{,}000 - 7{,}20{,}000 = 1{,}60{,}000 \text{ kWh/month}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Warning):** Many students calculate absolute saving as simply $E_b - E_c = 80{,}000$ kWh, forgetting that production has increased. This **normalisation** step is what differentiates a verified saving from a coincidental drop. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this mistake.

**Annualised savings (extra credit):**
$$
\Delta E_{annual} = 1{,}60{,}000 \times 12 = 19{,}20{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr} = 19.2 \text{ lakh kWh/yr}
$$

At ₹7/kWh, this translates to **₹13.44 lakh per year** in verified savings.

---

### Question B: IPMVP Protocols and M&V Plan for a Food Industry Boiler Retrofit

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Module 3 Internal Choice Option B]**

**(a)** Describe the four IPMVP M&V Options (A, B, C, D) with their respective accuracy levels and suitable applications in the food industry. Identify the most appropriate IPMVP Option for a steam boiler retrofit in a spice processing plant and justify your selection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Develop a complete Measurement and Verification (M&V) plan for the steam boiler retrofit. The plan should cover: measurement boundary, baseline determination, measurement points, instruments and accuracy, M&V period, and reporting protocol. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution (Question B)

#### Part (a) — IPMVP Four Options (7 Marks)

**Option A — Retrofit Isolation: Key Parameter Measurement (1.5 marks):**
- **What is measured:** One key parameter (typically kW or kWh of the retrofitted equipment)
- **Non-key parameters:** Stipulated from manufacturer specifications, nameplate data, or engineering estimates
- **Accuracy:** ±20% (lowest)
- **Cost:** Low
- **Food industry application:** VFD retrofit on a feed mill motor; LED lighting replacement in a bakery; replacement of single evaporator with energy-efficient unit

**Option B — Retrofit Isolation: All Parameter Measurement (1.5 marks):**
- **What is measured:** All key parameters — energy, output, and operational variables
- **Accuracy:** ±10%
- **Cost:** Medium to high
- **Food industry application:** Steam boiler retrofit in a spice plant; compressed air system optimisation in a juice plant; CIP system upgrade

**Option C — Whole Facility (1.5 marks):**
- **What is measured:** Energy use at the whole-facility level (main meter)
- **Uses EnB** with regression-based normalisation
- **Accuracy:** ±10%
- **Food industry application:** Multi-measure plant-wide retrofit combining lighting + HVAC + process + refrigeration; large dairy plant energy efficiency project

**Option D — Calibrated Simulation (1.5 marks):**
- **What is measured:** No direct physical measurement of savings; instead, a computer model is calibrated to match actual utility data, then used to predict baseline
- **Accuracy:** ±5% to ±15% (model dependent)
- **Food industry application:** New greenfield dairy or brewery plant; complex multi-process retrofits where baseline cannot be measured directly

**Most Appropriate Option for Spice Plant Boiler Retrofit (1 mark):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selected Option: IPMVP Option B.** Justification: A steam boiler retrofit has well-defined physical boundaries (fuel in, steam out). All parameters can be measured directly using flow meters, temperature sensors, and a fuel mass-flow meter. Option B delivers ±10% accuracy at moderate cost, which is appropriate for the ROI expectations of a food industry SME in Kerala.

#### Part (b) — Complete M&V Plan (7 Marks)

**1. Measurement Boundary (1 mark):**
The M&V boundary is the steam boiler system including fuel supply line, feedwater line, steam header, and condensate return. All energy crossing this boundary is metered.

**2. Baseline Determination (1.5 marks):**
- **Baseline period:** Minimum 12 months of operating data prior to retrofit (typical: 1 year)
- **Variables to normalise:** Production output (tonnes of spices processed), moisture content of feed (affects drying load), steam pressure setpoint, feedwater temperature
- **Statistical technique:** Linear regression to develop EnB equation $E_b = a_0 + a_1 \cdot Q + a_2 \cdot T_{ambient}$
- **Minimum data points:** 12 monthly data points

**3. Measurement Points (1.5 marks):**

| Measurement Point | Variable | Instrument | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fuel inlet | Mass flow rate of biomass | Coriolis flow meter | 0–500 kg/hr |
| Feedwater inlet | Temperature, mass flow | RTD + magnetic flow meter | 0–100°C, 0–50 m³/hr |
| Steam outlet | Mass flow, pressure, temperature | Vortex flow meter + PT + RTD | 0–20 TPH, 0–10 bar, 0–300°C |
| Stack | Flue gas temperature, O₂ | Portable flue gas analyser | 0–400°C, 0–21% O₂ |
| Production | Output rate | Weighbridge / flow meter | 0–50 TPH |

**4. Instruments and Accuracy (1 mark):**
- Class 0.5 kWh meter on motor auxiliaries
- Coriolis meter accuracy: ±0.1%
- Vortex meter accuracy: ±1%
- Temperature: PT100 RTD, Class A (±0.15°C @ 0°C)
- Annual calibration against traceable standards (NABL accredited lab)

**5. M&V Period (1 mark):**
- **Reporting period:** Monthly
- **M&V performance period:** Minimum 12 months post-retrofit
- **Long-term verification:** Quarterly CUSUM analysis to detect drift

**6. Reporting Protocol (1 mark):**
- Monthly Energy Performance Report to Plant Manager and Corporate Energy Cell
- Quarterly report to Managing Director with verified savings and ROI
- Annual third-party verification by certified M&V professional (CMVP)
- Documentation retained for minimum 3 years (per ISO 50001 Clause 7.5)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in M&V Plan Questions:**
> 1. Students forget to specify the **boundary** — examiner deducts 1 mark
> 2. Students mention instruments without specifying **accuracy class** — examiner deducts 1 mark
> 3. Students write "calibrate regularly" without specifying **frequency and standard** — examiner deducts 1 mark
> 4. Students omit **reporting frequency** — examiner deducts 1 mark
> 5. Students confuse IPMVP Options (e.g., describing Option A features under Option B)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU Board Exam Checklist — Module 3: MMV for EnMS Effectiveness**

- [x] **Definition of MMV** — systematic process of collecting, analysing, and converting energy data into actionable performance information
- [x] **ISO 50001 reference** — Clause 9.1 (Monitoring, Measurement, Analysis and Evaluation)
- [x] **EnB** = Energy Baseline; established using historical data normalised for significant variables
- [x] **EnPI** = Energy Performance Indicator; e.g., kWh/tonne, kg steam/kg product
- [x] **SEC formula:** $\text{SEC} = E / Q$
- [x] **% EnPI formula:** $(\text{SEC}_b - \text{SEC}_c)/\text{SEC}_b \times 100$
- [x] **Absolute energy saving:** $\Delta E = E_{b,adjusted} - E_{actual}$ (always use **adjusted** baseline)
- [x] **IPMVP Options A, B, C, D** — accuracy levels ±20%, ±10%, ±10%, ±5–15% respectively
- [x] **Food industry EnPIs:** kWh/tonne milk, kg steam/kg powder, COP of refrigeration
- [x] **Conversion:** 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ
- [x] **CUSUM** = Cumulative Sum control chart for drift detection
- [x] **Boiler efficiency formula:** $\eta = [m_s(h_s - h_{fw})/(m_f \cdot GCV)] \times 100$
- [x] **COP formula:** $Q_{cooling}/W_{electric}$
- [x] **MMV cycle = CHECK phase of PDCA** in ISO 50001
- [x] **Re-baseline only on major change** (>20% capacity expansion or process change)
- [x] **Calibration** — annual, NABL-traceable, class 0.5 or better for revenue-grade meters
- [x] **Verification = confirming actual savings** vs. claimed savings using M&V protocol
- [x] **Option B is the most common** in Indian food industry SME retrofits (boilers, compressors)
- [x] **Regression-based normalisation** is essential when production volume changes between baseline and post-retrofit periods

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Hook for IPMVP:**
> - **A** = "**A** few measurements, others **A**ssumed"
> - **B** = "**B**etter, measure **B**oth sides (all parameters)"
> - **C** = "**C**omplete facility meter"
> - **D** = "**D**esktop simulation, calibrated to **D**ata"

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies on EnMS Implementation and Certification in the Food Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INSUSTRY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Case Studies on EnMS Implementation and Certification in the Food Industry # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Energy Management System (EnMS)? An **Energy Management System (EnMS)** is a structured, documented, and continually improving management framework that allows an organization — specifically a food processing unit — to take a systematic approach to achieving continual improvement of **energy performance**, including **energy efficiency**, **energy use**, and **energy consumption**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):** *An Energy Management System is a set of interrelated or interacting elements to establish an energy policy and energy objectives, and the processes and procedures to achieve those objectives, in accordance with the ISO 50001 international standard.* The reference international standard governing EnMS is **ISO 50001:2018** — *"Energy management systems — Requirements with guidance for use"*. It supersedes the earlier ISO 50001:2011. ## 1.2 What is EnMS Certification? **EnMS Certification** is the formal, third-party audit-based attestation by an accredited Certification Body (CB) that an organization's energy management system conforms to all the mandatory clauses (Clauses 4–10) of **ISO 50001:2018**. Successful certification results in the issuance of an **ISO 50001 certificate** valid for **3 years**, subject to annual surveillance audits. ## 1.3 The Need for EnMS in the Food Industry The food industry is one of the most **energy-intensive** manufacturing sectors globally, accounting for roughly **30% of global industrial energy consumption**. Key drivers for adopting EnMS in food processing include: - High thermal energy demand for cooking, baking, pasteurization, sterilization, drying, and evaporation. - High electrical energy demand for refrigeration, cold-chain logistics, motors, pumps, fans, compressed air, and lighting. - Escalating cost of fossil fuels and grid electricity. - Carbon-emission reduction commitments and **Net-Zero 2070** targets. - Customer and retailer (e.g., Walmart, Tesco, IKEA) **Scope-3 emission** reporting demands. - Compliance with the **Energy Conservation Act (EC Act)**, **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme**, and BEE Star Rating for industries. ## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Health Regimen for a Factory" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Think of an EnMS exactly like a *personal health regimen* for a factory. > > 1. **Doctor's check-up** = Initial **Energy Review (Clause 6.3)** to identify "energy ailments." > 2. **Prescribed diet & exercise plan** = **Energy Objectives, Targets, and Action Plans (Clause 6.2)**. > 3. **Daily routine / discipline** = **Operational Controls (Clause 8.1)** to follow the plan. > 4. **Weighing scale & blood report** = **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs) and Monitoring (Clause 9.1)**. > 5. **Quarterly doctor visit** = **Internal Audit (Clause 9.2)** and **Management Review (Clause 9.3)**. > 6. **Re-assessment each year** = **Continual Improvement (Clause 10.3)**. > > Just as a person without a regimen can deteriorate, a factory without an EnMS will keep wasting energy and money. ## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU Board Exam Favorites) | Term | Meaning (as per ISO 50001:2018) | | :--- | :--- | | **EnMS** | Energy Management System | | **EnPI** | Energy Performance Indicator — a quantitative measure of energy performance | | **EnB** | Energy Baseline — quantitative reference for energy performance | | **SEU** | Significant Energy Use — energy use accounting for substantial consumption / savings potential | | **Energy Review** | Determination of current energy performance, SEUs, and improvement opportunities | | **Energy Performance** | Measurable result(s) related to energy efficiency, energy use, and energy consumption | | **M&V** | Measurement and Verification — quantifying savings using IPMVP protocols | | **CxO Commitment** | Top-management commitment — Clause 5 leadership requirements | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 ISO 50001:2018 — Clause-Wise Architecture The ISO 50001:2018 standard is built on the **Annex SL High-Level Structure (HLS)** — a 10-clause universal framework shared by all modern ISO management system standards (ISO 9001, ISO 14001, ISO 45001). This makes integration with **Quality (QMS)** and **Environmental (EMS)** management systems seamless. | Clause | Title | Purpose in EnMS Context | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | **1** | Scope | Defines the boundaries of the EnMS. | | **2** | Normative References | References ISO 50002 (audit), ISO 50003 (certification), ISO 50004–50015. | | **3** | Terms & Definitions | Defines EnMS, EnPI, EnB, SEU, etc. | | **4** | Context of the Organization | Internal / external issues, needs of interested parties, scope of EnMS. | | **5** | Leadership | Top management commitment, energy policy, roles & responsibilities. | | **6** | Planning | Energy review, EnPIs, EnB, objectives, action plans, **energy risk & opportunity assessment**. | | **7** | Support | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information. | | **8** | Operation | Operational planning & control, design, procurement, **SEU control**. | | **9** | Performance Evaluation | Monitoring, measurement, analysis, evaluation, internal audit, management review. | | **10** | Improvement | Nonconformity, corrective action, **continual improvement**. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory clauses for certification: 4 to 10.** Clauses 1–3 are *informative*. A missing Clause 4–10 requirement = automatic **Major Nonconformity** and rejection of certification. ## 2.2 The PDCA Cycle in EnMS ISO 50001 follows the classic **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle, perfectly aligning with NEP-2020 outcome-based learning: - **Plan (Clauses 4, 5, 6):** Establish context, leadership, energy policy, energy review, EnB/EnPI, objectives, and action plans. - **Do (Clauses 7, 8):** Provide resources, competence, awareness, communication; implement operational controls and SEU management. - **Check (Clause 9):** Monitor, measure, analyze EnPIs; conduct internal audits; hold management review. - **Act (Clause 10):** Address nonconformities, drive continual improvement. ## 2.3 EnMS Certification — The 3-Stage Audit Process Stage-1 Audit (Documentation Review): The Certification Body (CB) auditor reviews all **mandatory documented information** (energy policy, energy objectives, EnB, EnPI, energy review report, competence matrix, audit reports, management review minutes). Any gap = *finding* to be closed. Stage-2 Audit (On-site Certification Audit): On-site verification of implementation, operational control, SEU management, and competence. Auditor selects **SEUs** and traces them through the system. Stage-3: Annual Surveillance + Re-certification (every 3 years): Confirms continued conformance and continual improvement. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following formulas appear frequently in KTU Part B (14-mark) EnMS questions, viva voce, and energy audit computations. | Symbol | Formula | Description | Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{M_{prod}}$ | Total energy used per unit of production (e.g., kWh per tonne of milk processed). | $\dfrac{\text{kWh}}{\text{tonne}}$ | | **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** | $EnPI = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{actual}}$ | Ratio of energy consumed to a production variable. | $\dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{unit}}$ | | **Energy Savings (kWh/year)** | $ES = E_{baseline} - E_{reported}$ | Difference between baseline and reporting-period energy use. | kWh / year | | **Percentage Energy Savings** | $\%ES = \left( \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{reported}}{E_{baseline}} \right) \times 100$ | Normalized percentage reduction in energy. | \% | | **Energy Cost Savings (₹)** | $ECS = ES \times C_{unit}$ | Monetary value of energy saved. | ₹ / year | | **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** | $SPP = \dfrac{I_{capex}}{ECS}$ | Time to recover the capital investment. | years | | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | $ROI = \dfrac{ECS}{I_{capex}} \times 100$ | Annualized return on the energy-efficiency investment. | \% | | **Carbon Dioxide Equivalent Reduction** | $CO_{2e} = ES \times EF_{grid}$ | Where **EF\_grid = 0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh** (CEA 2023, India grid average). | tonnes CO₂e | | **CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) Drift** | $CUSUM = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (E_{i} - \overline{E})$ | Detects long-term drift in EnPI. | kWh | | **Normalised Baseline Regression** | $E_{adj} = a \cdot P + b$ | Linear EnB model — energy adjusted for production level. | kWh | > **Engineering Constants (Indian context, memorize for KTU exams):** > - **Grid Emission Factor (India, CEA 2023):** **0.716 kg CO₂e / kWh** > - **NCV of furnace oil:** **10,200 kcal/kg** (= 42.47 MJ/kg) > - **NCV of natural gas:** **8,500 kcal/m³** (= 35.59 MJ/m³) > - **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal** ## 2.5 Utility of EnMS in Food Industry Engineering 1. **Cost reduction** — typical 5–25% energy-cost reduction in 2 years. 2. **Regulatory compliance** — EC Act, PAT scheme, EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) for packaging. 3. **Brand value** — ISO 50001 certification improves ESG ratings. 4. **Supply-chain access** — many MNCs (Nestlé, Unilever, PepsiCo) now mandate ISO 50001 from their suppliers. 5. **Carbon trading** — generates **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)** under the PAT scheme. 6. **Integration** — easy to merge with **ISO 14001 (EMS)** and **ISO 9001 (QMS)** into an **Integrated Management System (IMS)**. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — EnPI Calculation for a Dairy Plant (14-Mark Standard) > **Problem:** A dairy plant processing **50,000 tonnes/year of milk** consumed **18,000 MWh** of electricity and **3,000 tonnes of furnace oil** in the reporting year. In the baseline year, the same plant processed **45,000 tonnes/year of milk** and consumed **20,000 MWh** of electricity and **3,300 tonnes of furnace oil**. Calculate: > (a) Baseline SEC, (b) Reporting SEC, (c) Energy savings in MWh and percentage, (d) Annual cost saving (assume ₹8/kWh blended tariff and ₹65/kg furnace oil), (e) Tonnes of CO₂e avoided. ### Step 1 — Convert all energy to a common unit (MJ). $$E_{electricity,\;baseline} = 20{,}000 \; \text{MWh} \times 3.6 \; \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{kWh}} \times 1000 = 7.20 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{furnace\;oil,\;baseline} = 3{,}300 \; \text{tonnes} \times 1000 \; \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{tonne}} \times 42.47 \; \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{kg}} = 1.4015 \times 10^{8} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{total,\;baseline} = 7.20 \times 10^{7} + 1.4015 \times 10^{8} = 2.1215 \times 10^{8} \; \text{MJ}$$ ### Step 2 — Baseline Specific Energy Consumption (SEC). $$SEC_{baseline} = \dfrac{2.1215 \times 10^{8} \; \text{MJ}}{45{,}000 \; \text{tonnes}} = 4{,}714.4 \; \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{tonne\;milk}}$$ ### Step 3 — Reporting year energy in MJ. $$E_{electricity,\;report} = 18{,}000 \times 3.6 \times 1000 = 6.48 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{furnace\;oil,\;report} = 3{,}000 \times 1000 \times 42.47 = 1.2741 \times 10^{8} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{total,\;report} = 6.48 \times 10^{7} + 1.2741 \times 10^{8} = 1.9221 \times 10^{8} \; \text{MJ}$$ ### Step 4 — Reporting SEC. $$SEC_{report} = \dfrac{1.9221 \times 10^{8}}{50{,}000} = 3{,}844.2 \; \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{tonne\;milk}}$$ ### Step 5 — Absolute energy savings. $$\Delta E = E_{total,\;baseline} - E_{total,\;report} = 2.1215 \times 10^{8} - 1.9221 \times 10^{8} = 1.994 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ Converting to MWh: $$\Delta E_{MWh} = \dfrac{1.994 \times 10^{7}}{3.6 \times 10^{3}} = 5{,}538.9 \; \text{MWh}$$ ### Step 6 — Percentage energy savings. $$\%ES = \left( \dfrac{2.1215 \times 10^{8} - 1.9221 \times 10^{8}}{2.1215 \times 10^{8}} \right) \times 100 = 9.40 \;\%$$ ### Step 7 — Annual cost savings (electricity + furnace oil). $$ECS_{elec} = (20{,}000 - 18{,}000) \; \text{MWh} \times 1000 \times 8 = ₹1{,}60{,}00{,}000 = ₹1.60 \; \text{Crore}$$ $$ECS_{oil} = (3{,}300 - 3{,}000) \times 1000 \times 65 = ₹1{,}95{,}00{,}000 = ₹1.95 \; \text{Crore}$$ $$ECS_{total} = ₹1.60 + ₹1.95 = ₹3.55 \; \text{Crore / year}$$ ### Step 8 — CO₂ equivalent avoided (using Indian grid EF = 0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh). $$CO_{2e} = 5{,}538.9 \times 1000 \times 0.716 = 3{,}965{,}852 \; \text{kg} \approx 3{,}966 \; \text{tonnes \; CO}_{2}\text{e}$$ ### Step 9 — Tabulated result. | Metric | Baseline Year | Reporting Year | Savings | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Milk processed (tonnes) | 45,000 | 50,000 | — | | Total energy (MJ) | $2.1215 \times 10^{8}$ | $1.9221 \times 10^{8}$ | $1.994 \times 10^{7}$ MJ | | SEC (MJ/tonne) | 4,714.4 | 3,844.2 | 870.2 MJ/tonne | | Cost (₹ Crore) | 4.55 | 4.55 ≈ | ₹3.55 Crore | | CO₂e (tonnes) | 14,320 | 12,930 | **3,966 t CO₂e** | ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Linear Regression EnB for a Beverage Plant > **Problem:** A beverage plant has the following historical annual data. Develop the **Energy Baseline (EnB)** linear regression model $E = a \cdot P + b$ and predict the energy use at a future production level of **60,000 kL**. | Year | Production P (kL) | Energy E (MWh) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Y1 | 30,000 | 12,000 | | Y2 | 35,000 | 13,500 | | Y3 | 40,000 | 14,800 | | Y4 | 45,000 | 16,300 | | Y5 | 50,000 | 17,700 | ### Step 1 — Compute means. $$\bar{P} = \dfrac{30{,}000 + 35{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 45{,}000 + 50{,}000}{5} = 40{,}000 \; \text{kL}$$ $$\bar{E} = \dfrac{12{,}000 + 13{,}500 + 14{,}800 + 16{,}300 + 17{,}700}{5} = 14{,}860 \; \text{MWh}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute slope **a**. $$a = \dfrac{\sum (P_i - \bar{P})(E_i - \bar{E})}{\sum (P_i - \bar{P})^2}$$ Compute term by term: $$(30{,}000 - 40{,}000)(12{,}000 - 14{,}860) = (-10{,}000)(-2{,}860) = 2.86 \times 10^{7}$$ $$(35{,}000 - 40{,}000)(13{,}500 - 14{,}860) = (-5{,}000)(-1{,}360) = 6.80 \times 10^{6}$$ $$(40{,}000 - 40{,}000)(14{,}800 - 14{,}860) = 0$$ $$(45{,}000 - 40{,}000)(16{,}300 - 14{,}860) = (5{,}000)(1{,}440) = 7.20 \times 10^{6}$$ $$(50{,}000 - 40{,}000)(17{,}700 - 14{,}860) = (10{,}000)(2{,}840) = 2.84 \times 10^{7}$$ $$\sum (P_i - \bar{P})(E_i - \bar{E}) = 2.86 \times 10^{7} + 6.80 \times 10^{6} + 0 + 7.20 \times 10^{6} + 2.84 \times 10^{7} = 7.30 \times 10^{7}$$ Sum of squared deviations: $$\sum (P_i - \bar{P})^2 = 10^{8} + 2.5 \times 10^{7} + 0 + 2.5 \times 10^{7} + 10^{8} = 2.5 \times 10^{8}$$ $$a = \dfrac{7.30 \times 10^{7}}{2.5 \times 10^{8}} = 0.292 \; \dfrac{\text{MWh}}{\text{kL}}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute intercept **b**. $$b = \bar{E} - a \cdot \bar{P} = 14{,}860 - (0.292)(40{,}000) = 14{,}860 - 11{,}680 = 3{,}180 \; \text{MWh}$$ ### Step 4 — Final EnB model. $$E_{adj} = 0.292 \cdot P + 3{,}180 \; \text{MWh}$$ ### Step 5 — Predicted energy at P = 60,000 kL. $$E_{pred} = 0.292 \times 60{,}000 + 3{,}180 = 17{,}520 + 3{,}180 = 20{,}700 \; \text{MWh}$$ If the plant actually consumes only **18,000 MWh** at that production level, the normalized savings are: $$E_{saved} = 20{,}700 - 18{,}000 = 2{,}700 \; \text{MWh/year}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask *"Why use a regression-based EnB instead of a single-year baseline?"* — The single-year baseline fails when production varies; the regression EnB **normalises** energy to a common production level, giving a fair comparison. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ISO 50001:2018 EnMS — Full Implementation Flow (PDCA) ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["Step 1 Context Analysis
Clause 4 Identify Issues
Interested Parties Scope"] node2["Step 2 Leadership
Clause 5 Energy Policy
Top Management Commitment"] node3["Step 3 Energy Review
Clause 6.3 SEU EnB EnPI"] node4["Step 4 Objectives and
Action Plans Clause 6.2"] node5["Step 5 Support Resources
Competence Awareness
Communication Clause 7"] node6["Step 6 Operational Control
SEU Management
Design Procurement Clause 8"] node7["Step 7 Monitoring and
Measurement EnPI
Internal Audit MR Clause 9"] node8["Step 8 Continual
Improvement NC CAPA
Clause 10"] node9["Mature EnMS
ISO 50001 Certified
Energy Performance Up"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6 node6 --> node7 node7 --> node8 node8 --> node9 node8 -.Feedback.-> node1 node7 -.Feedback.-> node3 ``` ## 4.2 Three-Stage Certification Audit Process ```mermaid flowchart LR stageA["Stage 1 Documentation
Review by CB Auditor
Mandatory Clauses 4 to 10"] stageB["Stage 2 On Site
Certification Audit
SEU Trace Operational"] stageC["Stage 3 Annual
Surveillance Plus
3 Year Recertification"] cert["ISO 50001 Certificate
Issued Valid 3 Years"] stageA -->|Any Major NC| ncFix1["Close NC Within 90 Days"] ncFix1 --> stageA stageA -->|Compliant| stageB stageB -->|Any Major NC| ncFix2["Close NC Within 90 Days"] ncFix2 --> stageB stageB -->|Compliant| cert cert --> stageC stageC -->|Any Major NC| ncFix3["Close NC Within 30 Days"] ncFix3 --> stageC stageC -.Recert Every 3 Yr.-> stageA ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture of an EnMS in a Food Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD top["Top Management
Energy Policy and Leadership"] enr["Energy Manager

Production Maintenance Utilities
QA Purchase Finance"] seumodule["SEU Registry
Boilers Compressors
Refrigeration Motors HVAC"] meter["Smart Metering and
SCADA Energy Data

CUSUM Drift Detection"] audit["Internal Audit and
Management Review
