System ISO 9001 14001
45001 Interface"] top --> enr enr --> team team --> seumodule seumodule --> meter meter --> enpi enpi --> audit audit --> ims ims -.Continual Improvement.-> top ``` ## 4.4 Typical EnMS Project Timeline (12–18 Months) ```mermaid gantt title EnMS Implementation Timeline Food Industry dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD axisFormat %b section Plan Gap Analysis and Context :a1, 2024-01-01, 45d Energy Policy and Leadership :a2, after a1, 30d Energy Review and SEU Mapping :a3, after a2, 60d EnB and EnPI Definition :a4, after a3, 30d section Do Objectives Targets and Action Plans :b1, after a4, 45d Competence Training and Awareness :b2, after b1, 60d Operational Controls and SOPs :b3, after b2, 60d section Check Monitoring and Measurement :c1, after b3, 60d Internal Audit :c2, after c1, 30d Management Review :c3, after c2, 15d section Act Stage 1 Documentation Audit :d1, after c3, 15d Stage 2 On Site Certification Audit :d2, after d1, 30d Certification Issued :milestone, after d2, 0d ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] Define Energy Management System (EnMS) and state any four mandatory documented information required by ISO 50001:2018. **Model Answer:** An **Energy Management System (EnMS)** is a set of interrelated or interacting elements used to establish an energy policy and energy objectives, and the processes and procedures to achieve those objectives, as defined by **ISO 50001:2018**. Four mandatory documented information requirements: 1. **Scope of the EnMS** (Clause 4.3) — boundaries and applicability. 2. **Energy Policy** (Clause 5.2) — commitment to continual improvement. 3. **Energy Review Report** (Clause 6.3) — SEUs, EnB, EnPI determination. 4. **Energy Objectives and Action Plans** (Clause 6.2) — measurable targets with responsibilities, resources, and timelines. *(Other acceptable: roles/responsibilities, criteria for evaluation of energy performance, internal audit programme, results of management review.)* ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Differentiate between Energy Baseline (EnB) and Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI). Why is a regression-based EnB preferred over a single-year baseline? **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Energy Baseline (EnB) | Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Definition | Quantitative reference that serves as a comparison point for energy performance. | Quantitative measure of energy performance as defined by the organization. | | Purpose | Defines *what "normal" was* before improvement actions. | Tracks *current performance* against the baseline. | | Form | A line, equation, or fixed number. | A ratio (e.g., MJ/tonne, kWh/kl). | | Update | Reviewed and updated when SEUs, processes, or EnPI change. | Monitored continuously. | **Why regression EnB is preferred:** A single-year baseline is **sensitive to production volume**; if production falls, energy falls too, falsely showing "savings." A regression-based EnB mathematically **normalises** energy to a chosen production variable, isolating *true* efficiency improvements from volume effects. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper] (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle as applied to ISO 50001:2018 EnMS. Map each PDCA stage to the relevant ISO 50001 clauses. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A bakery producing **20,000 tonnes/year of bread** consumed **6,500 MWh** of electricity and **500 tonnes of LPG** in the baseline year. After implementing EnMS-driven measures (VFD on motors, heat recovery from oven exhaust, LED lighting), the same plant produced **22,000 tonnes/year** and consumed **5,800 MWh** of electricity and **420 tonnes of LPG**. Calculate the **(i) baseline and reporting SEC in MJ/tonne, (ii) energy savings in MWh, (iii) percentage savings, (iv) annual cost saving (₹9/kWh, ₹95/kg LPG)**, and **(v) tonnes of CO₂e avoided (grid EF = 0.716 kg/kWh; NCV of LPG = 47 MJ/kg)**. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) — PDCA Mapping | PDCA Stage | ISO 50001:2018 Clauses | Activities | | :---: | :---: | :--- | | **Plan** | 4, 5, 6 | Context analysis, leadership, energy policy, energy review (SEU, EnB, EnPI), objectives, action plans, risk & opportunity assessment. | | **Do** | 7, 8 | Resources, competence, awareness, communication, documented information; operational controls, design, procurement, SEU management. | | **Check** | 9 | Monitoring & measurement of EnPIs, evaluation of compliance, internal audit, management review. | | **Act** | 10 | Nonconformity and corrective action, continual improvement of energy performance. | The PDCA cycle ensures **continual improvement** and prepares the plant for **ISO 50001 certification**. #### Model Solution — Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Step 1 — Convert baseline energy to MJ.** $$E_{elec,\;bl} = 6{,}500 \times 1000 \times 3.6 = 2.34 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{LPG,\;bl} = 500 \times 1000 \times 47 = 2.35 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{total,\;bl} = 2.34 \times 10^{7} + 2.35 \times 10^{7} = 4.69 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ **Step 2 — Baseline SEC.** $$SEC_{bl} = \dfrac{4.69 \times 10^{7}}{20{,}000} = 2{,}345 \; \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{tonne}}$$ **Step 3 — Reporting energy in MJ.** $$E_{elec,\;rep} = 5{,}800 \times 1000 \times 3.6 = 2.088 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{LPG,\;rep} = 420 \times 1000 \times 47 = 1.974 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$E_{total,\;rep} = 2.088 \times 10^{7} + 1.974 \times 10^{7} = 4.062 \times 10^{7} \; \text{MJ}$$ **Step 4 — Reporting SEC.** $$SEC_{rep} = \dfrac{4.062 \times 10^{7}}{22{,}000} = 1{,}846.4 \; \dfrac{\text{MJ}}{\text{tonne}}$$ **Step 5 — Energy savings in MJ and MWh.** $$\Delta E = 4.69 \times 10^{7} - 4.062 \times 10^{7} = 6.28 \times 10^{6} \; \text{MJ}$$ $$\Delta E_{MWh} = \dfrac{6.28 \times 10^{6}}{3.6 \times 10^{3}} = 1{,}744.4 \; \text{MWh}$$ **Step 6 — Percentage energy savings.** $$\%ES = \dfrac{6.28 \times 10^{6}}{4.69 \times 10^{7}} \times 100 = 13.39 \;\%$$ **Step 7 — Cost savings.** $$ECS_{elec} = (6{,}500 - 5{,}800) \times 1000 \times 9 = ₹63{,}00{,}000 = ₹63 \; \text{Lakh}$$ $$ECS_{LPG} = (500 - 420) \times 1000 \times 95 = ₹76{,}00{,}000 = ₹76 \; \text{Lakh}$$ $$ECS_{total} = ₹63 + ₹76 = ₹1.39 \; \text{Crore / year}$$ **Step 8 — CO₂e avoided (electricity only — electricity is grid-connected).** $$CO_{2e} = (6{,}500 - 5{,}800) \times 1000 \times 0.716 = 5{,}012 \; \text{tonnes CO}_{2}\text{e / year}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Do *not* convert CO₂e for LPG/LNG/natural gas using the grid emission factor of 0.716. LPG is a *fuel*, and its CO₂e must be calculated from its **NCV (47 MJ/kg) × emission factor (~63 kg CO₂/GJ of fuel)**. Using the grid EF for fuels is a common mistake costing 1–2 marks. --- ### Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper] (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Q-A* **(a)** Discuss **five case studies** of EnMS implementation in different food industry sub-sectors (dairy, bakery, brewery, edible oil, sugar). Highlight the SEUs identified, EnPIs used, and the percentage energy saved in each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **three-stage certification audit process** of ISO 50001. List any **eight mandatory documented information** that a food company must maintain to be eligible for certification. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) — Five Food Industry Case Studies | Sub-sector | Plant (illustrative) | SEU Identified | EnPI Used | Energy Saved | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | | **Dairy (Amul, Banas, Mother Dairy)** | 10 Lakh LPD dairy | Pasteurizer, chilling, boiler, CIP | kWh / tonne milk | **18 – 22 %** | | **Bakery (Britannia, ITC, Parle)** | Bread & biscuit plant | Tunnel oven, dough mixer, refrigeration | MJ / tonne product | **12 – 15 %** | | **Brewery (Kingfisher, United Breweries)** | 1 lakh KL/yr plant | Boil kettle, fermenter, glycol chiller, CO₂ compressor | MJ / kL beer | **10 – 14 %** | | **Edible Oil (Adani Wilmar, Cargill)** | Solvent extraction | De-solventizer, steam economy, dryer | MJ / tonne oil | **14 – 18 %** | | **Sugar (EID Parry, Balrampur Chini)** | 5,000 TCD plant | Cane preparation, evaporators, centrifuges, boilers | MJ / tonne sugar | **8 – 12 %** | **Key SEU common across food plants:** 1. **Steam boiler + turbine cogeneration** (largest fuel consumer). 2. **Ammonia / Freon refrigeration plant** (largest electricity consumer). 3. **Compressed air system** (often 30% leaky — huge saving potential). 4. **Motors + pumps + fans** (Variable Frequency Drives / VFDs). 5. **Heat recovery** from exhaust flue gases, hot condensate, and oven exhaust. #### Model Solution — Part (b) — Certification Audit & Documented Information **Three-Stage Certification Audit (Clause references):** 1. **Stage 1 — Documentation Review (Readiness Review):** Auditor verifies all Clauses 4–10 documented information is in place; checks energy review, EnB, EnPI, and that internal audit and management review have been completed at least once. Result: *ready / not ready for Stage 2*. 2. **Stage 2 — On-site Certification Audit:** Auditor walks the plant, interviews staff, traces SEUs through the system, verifies operational control, competence, monitoring, and CAPA effectiveness. Result: *recommend / not recommend certification*. 3. **Stage 3 — Surveillance (annually) and Re-certification (every 3 years):** Confirms continual improvement, addresses minor NCs, and re-validates the EnB. **Eight Mandatory Documented Information Items:** 1. Scope of the EnMS (Clause 4.3). 2. Energy Policy (Clause 5.2). 3. Roles, responsibilities, and authorities (Clause 5.3). 4. Results of the **Energy Review** (Clause 6.3) — SEUs, EnB, EnPI. 5. Energy Objectives and Action Plans (Clause 6.2). 6. Criteria and methodology for **EnPI evaluation** (Clause 9.1.1). 7. Internal Audit Programme and Results (Clause 9.2). 8. Management Review Results (Clause 9.3). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write *only 4–5 items* in this question. A 14-mark question expects **8 items with proper clause numbers**. Writing numbers like "Clause 6.2" gets you full credit. Also note that, unlike ISO 14001, ISO 50001 has **no requirement for documented procedures** — only *documented information* (i.e., any format: paper, digital, video). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 30-second revision bullets before the exam:** - **EnMS** is governed by **ISO 50001:2018**, with 10 clauses (Clauses 4–10 are mandatory for certification). - **PDCA cycle** maps perfectly: Plan (4-6), Do (7-8), Check (9), Act (10). - **EnB (Energy Baseline)** = reference; **EnPI (Energy Performance Indicator)** = metric. Both are *mandatory*. - **SEU (Significant Energy Use)** = the energy uses that account for the majority of consumption and offer the greatest savings. - **3-Stage Certification** = Stage 1 docs review → Stage 2 on-site audit → Stage 3 annual surveillance + 3-year re-cert. - **Linear regression EnB** $E = aP + b$ is the most common normalisation model in food plants. - **India grid emission factor = 0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh (CEA 2023)** — use this in numerical questions. - **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal** — keep conversions handy. - **Typical food industry EnMS savings = 8–25%** over 2–3 years, with payback of 1–3 years. - **Energy Review (Clause 6.3)** must produce: energy consumption profile, SEUs, EnB, EnPI, and an Energy Action Plan. - **Common SEUs in food plants:** boilers, refrigeration, compressed air, motors/pumps, HVAC, ovens, driers, CIP systems. - **Continual improvement (Clause 10.3)** is the *heart* of ISO 50001 — every year the EnPI must show measurable improvement. - **Internal Audit (Clause 9.2)** + **Management Review (Clause 9.3)** must be conducted at least once before certification. - **Integrate** ISO 50001 with ISO 9001 (QMS) and ISO 14001 (EMS) using the common Annex SL structure — saves documentation effort. - **Indian regulatory drivers:** EC Act 2001, **PAT Scheme** (BEE), ESG reporting (SEBI BRSR), Net-Zero 2070. - **Certification cycle:** 3 years; surveillance audits every 12 months. - **Major NC** in Stage 2 = certificate *not issued*; **Minor NC** = closed within 90 days. > **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Document it. Measure it. Improve it. Repeat it."* — This is the ISO 50001 way of life in every certified food plant.

topics.title: Assessment of Carbon Footprint in Food Processing Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INSUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Assessment of Carbon Footprint in Food Processing Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carbon Footprint (CF) in Food Processing** is formally defined as the **total mass of Greenhouse Gases (GHGs)** expressed in **Carbon Dioxide equivalent (CO₂e)** units, emitted directly or indirectly throughout the **life cycle of a food product** — from agricultural raw material production, through processing, packaging, distribution, consumption, and end-of-life disposal.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$
CF_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (AD_i \times EF_i \times GWP_i)
$$

Where:
- $AD_i$ = Activity Data (e.g., kWh of electricity, litres of diesel, kg of waste)
- $EF_i$ = Emission Factor (kg CO₂e per unit activity)
- $GWP_i$ = Global Warming Potential of the $i^{th}$ GHG (dimensionless multiplier)

The **standard reference unit** is **kg CO₂e / tonne of finished product** or **kg CO₂e / kg of product**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Weight of Your Breath"

Imagine every food product you manufacture carries an invisible "**weight tag**" — not in kilograms of food, but in kilograms of CO₂ released to produce it.

- A **1 kg packet of biscuits** carries roughly **1.5–2.5 kg CO₂e** of hidden climate weight.
- A **1 litre of packaged milk** carries roughly **0.9–3.2 kg CO₂e**.

Just like a person on a weighing scale, the planet is "weighed down" by these accumulated emissions. **Carbon footprint assessment is therefore the act of putting the food industry on a planetary weighing scale.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Major GHGs covered under UNFCCC/Kyoto Protocol:**
> Carbon Dioxide (CO₂), Methane (CH₄), Nitrous Oxide (N₂O), Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs), Perfluorocarbons (PFCs), and Sulphur Hexafluoride (SF₆). For food processing, **CO₂, CH₄, and N₂O are most relevant**.

### 1.3 The Three Scopes of Carbon Footprint (GHG Protocol)

The **Greenhouse Gas Protocol Corporate Standard** classifies emissions in food processing into:

| Scope | Description | Examples in Food Industry |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **Scope 1 — Direct Emissions** | Owned/controlled sources | Boilers (natural gas), diesel generators, refrigerant leaks, on-site wastewater treatment |
| **Scope 2 — Indirect (Energy)** | Purchased electricity/steam/heat | Grid electricity for motors, lighting, refrigeration, compressed air |
| **Scope 3 — Value Chain Emissions** | Upstream + downstream indirect | Agricultural inputs (fertilizer, livestock), packaging materials, transport, retail refrigeration, consumer use |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a typical food processing plant, **Scope 3 contributes 60–80%** of total carbon footprint, **Scope 2 contributes 15–30%**, and **Scope 1 contributes 5–15%**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Standards & Frameworks for CF Assessment

The following international standards govern food carbon footprint assessment:

| Standard | Scope | Application |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **ISO 14064-1** | Organizational level | Quantify and report GHG inventories |
| **ISO 14064-2** | Project level | Quantify GHG reductions from specific projects |
| **ISO 14067** | Product level | Carbon footprint of products (CFP) |
| **PAS 2050** | Product level | Spec for assessment of goods/services life-cycle GHG |
| **GHG Protocol** | Corporate | Three-scope methodology |
| **Product Category Rules (PCR)** | Specific products | Defines rules for product-level CF |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Methodology (Cradle-to-Gate Approach)

A typical **Carbon Footprint Assessment (CFA)** in a food processing plant follows:

1. **Goal & Scope Definition** — Define product system, functional unit, reference flow.
2. **System Boundary Setting** — Cradle-to-gate, cradle-to-grave, or gate-to-gate.
3. **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)** — Collect all input/output data (energy, materials, waste).
4. **Emission Factor Selection** — Use national databases (e.g., DEFRA, IPCC, CEA).
5. **Impact Assessment** — Convert GHG to CO₂e via GWP.
6. **Interpretation** — Identify hotspots, propose reduction strategies.

### 2.3 Sources of Carbon Emissions in Food Processing

#### A. Direct (Scope 1) Sources:
- **Steam boilers** burning coal, furnace oil, natural gas, biomass.
- **Diesel forklifts** and **DG sets** for backup power.
- **Refrigerant leaks** from cold storage (HFC-R134a, R404a are very high GWP).
- **On-site effluent treatment** (CH₄ from anaerobic digesters).
- **Biomass combustion** in driers (CO₂ considered neutral if renewable).

#### B. Indirect (Scope 2) Sources:
- Purchased **grid electricity** — major contributor in bakery, dairy, beverage plants.
- Purchased **steam** from external cogeneration plants.

#### C. Indirect (Scope 3) Sources:
- **Agriculture**: fertilizer production (N₂O), enteric fermentation (CH₄), rice cultivation.
- **Packaging**: PET, aluminium, glass, paperboard production.
- **Transport**: inbound raw materials + outbound finished goods.
- **End-of-life**: landfill CH₄, packaging recycling emissions.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are critical for KTU ESE numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | $CO_2e = \sum (AD_i \times EF_i \times GWP_i)$ | Universal emission calculation |
| 2 | $E_{CO_2} = V_f \times NCV \times EF_{CO_2} \times 10^{-3}$ | CO₂ from fuel combustion (tonnes) |
| 3 | $E_{CH_4} = V_f \times NCV \times EF_{CH_4} \times 10^{-3}$ | CH₄ from combustion |
| 4 | $E_{N_2O} = V_f \times NCV \times EF_{N_2O} \times 10^{-3}$ | N₂O from combustion |
| 5 | $E_{elec} = E_{grid} \times EF_{grid}$ | Scope 2 emissions |
| 6 | $CF_{per\_unit} = \dfrac{CF_{total}}{M_{finished\_product}}$ | Per-kg or per-litre CF |
| 7 | $CF_{Scope\_1+2+3} = CF_1 + CF_2 + CF_3$ | Total corporate CF |
| 8 | $\eta_{reduction} = \dfrac{CF_{base} - CF_{new}}{CF_{base}} \times 100$ | % reduction in CF |
| 9 | $E_{refrigerant} = m_{leak} \times GWP_{ref}$ | Fugitive emissions from cooling |
| 10 | $E_{transit} = D \times M \times EF_{truck}$ | Transport emissions (kg CO₂e) |

### 2.5 Global Warming Potential (GWP-100) Reference Values

| Gas | Chemical Formula | GWP (100-year, AR5) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | **1** |
| Methane | CH₄ | **28** |
| Nitrous Oxide | N₂O | **265** |
| HFC-134a | CH₂FCF₃ | **1,300** |
| HFC-404a | Blend | **3,922** |
| Sulphur Hexafluoride | SF₆ | **23,500** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The values above use the **IPCC Fifth Assessment Report (AR5)** with **GWP-100 horizon**. Always check whether the question specifies **AR4 or AR5** — values differ significantly (e.g., CH₄ GWP = 25 in AR4 vs. 28 in AR5).

### 2.6 Standard Emission Factors (Indicative)

| Activity | Emission Factor (kg CO₂e per unit) |
|:---|:---:|
| Grid electricity (India, CEA 2022) | **0.716** kg CO₂e / kWh |
| Natural gas combustion | **2.02** kg CO₂e / m³ |
| Furnace oil | **3.20** kg CO₂e / litre |
| Diesel combustion | **2.68** kg CO₂e / litre |
| PNG combustion | **2.05** kg CO₂e / kg |
| Coal (Indian grade) | **2.42** kg CO₂e / kg |
| Truck transport (diesel) | **0.1067** kg CO₂e / tonne-km |
| Refrigerant R-134a leak | **1,300** kg CO₂e / kg |
| Water supply | **0.344** kg CO₂e / m³ |

> [!TIP]
> Always refer to the **latest Central Electricity Authority (CEA) baseline database** or **DEFRA conversion factors** for KTU numerical problems unless otherwise stated.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Carbon footprint assessment in food processing enables:
- **Regulatory compliance** with EU CBAM, India's Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS).
- **Eco-labelling** and **green marketing** for products.
- **Supply chain optimization** to reduce Scope 3 emissions.
- **Carbon-neutral certification** for premium products.
- **Identification of energy efficiency hotspots** — directly linking to energy management.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Dairy Processing Plant

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU Numerical Pattern):**
> A dairy plant processes **50,000 litres of milk per day** into pasteurized packaged milk. Annual data:
>
> - Furnace oil consumption: **180,000 litres/year**
> - Grid electricity: **1.2 million kWh/year**
> - Diesel (forklift + DG): **12,000 litres/year**
> - Refrigerant R-404a leakage: **18 kg/year**
> - Inbound raw milk transport: **150,000 tonne-km/year (diesel truck)**
>
> Compute the **total annual carbon footprint** and **per-litre CF**.

#### Step 1 — Identify Scope-Wise Emissions

- Furnace oil (boiler) → **Scope 1** (direct combustion)
- Diesel (forklift/DG) → **Scope 1**
- Refrigerant leak → **Scope 1** (fugitive)
- Grid electricity → **Scope 2**
- Inbound transport → **Scope 3**

#### Step 2 — Scope 1 Calculations

**A. Furnace Oil Combustion CO₂:**

$$
E_{CO_2}^{FO} = V_{FO} \times EF_{FO} = 180{,}000 \times 3.20 = 576{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**B. Diesel Combustion (Combustion only — CH₄ and N₂O negligible for liquid fuels in CO₂ terms):**

$$
E_{CO_2}^{diesel} = V_{diesel} \times EF_{diesel} = 12{,}000 \times 2.68 = 32{,}160 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**C. Refrigerant R-404a Leakage:**

$$
E_{ref} = m_{leak} \times GWP_{R404a} = 18 \times 3{,}922 = 70{,}596 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Total Scope 1:**

$$
CF_1 = 576{,}000 + 32{,}160 + 70{,}596 = 678{,}756 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

#### Step 3 — Scope 2 Calculations

**Grid Electricity Emissions:**

$$
E_{grid} = E_{kWh} \times EF_{grid} = 1{,}200{,}000 \times 0.716 = 859{,}200 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

$$
CF_2 = 859{,}200 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

#### Step 4 — Scope 3 Calculations

**Inbound Transport (Diesel Truck):**

$$
E_{transit} = D \times M \times EF_{truck} = 150{,}000 \times 0.1067 = 16{,}005 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

$$
CF_3 = 16{,}005 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

#### Step 5 — Total Annual Carbon Footprint

$$
CF_{total} = CF_1 + CF_2 + CF_3 = 678{,}756 + 859{,}200 + 16{,}005
$$

$$
\boxed{CF_{total} = 1{,}553{,}961 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e / year} \approx 1{,}554 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e / year}}
$$

#### Step 6 — Per-Unit (Per Litre) CF

Annual production:

$$
M_{annual} = 50{,}000 \times 365 = 18{,}250{,}000 \text{ litres / year}
$$

$$
CF_{per\_litre} = \dfrac{CF_{total}}{M_{annual}} = \dfrac{1{,}553{,}961}{18{,}250{,}000}
$$

$$
\boxed{CF_{per\_litre} = 0.0852 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e / litre}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Benchmarking Insight:** Global average for pasteurized packaged milk is **0.9–3.2 kg CO₂e/litre** (cradle-to-grave including farm). Our plant value of **0.085 kg CO₂e/litre** is **processing-only** (gate-to-gate), which is consistent with literature.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Bakery Plant Reduction Strategy

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A bakery plant has baseline $CF_{base} = 4{,}200$ tonnes CO₂e/year. After installing a biomass boiler (replacing 60% of furnace oil) and a 100 kWp solar PV (offsetting 12% grid power), calculate the **% reduction in carbon footprint** and **new CF**.

**Step 1 — Reduction from biomass boiler (replaces 60% FO):**

$$
\Delta CF_{biomass} = 0.60 \times 4{,}200 \times \alpha
$$

Assuming biomass displaces only the Scope-1 furnace-oil portion:

$$
\Delta CF_{biomass} = 0.60 \times 1{,}008 = 604.8 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e (avoided)}
$$

(Where 1,008 = Scope 1 share assumed = 24% of total.)

**Step 2 — Reduction from solar PV (12% of grid electricity):**

$$
\Delta CF_{solar} = 0.12 \times 0.30 \times 4{,}200 = 151.2 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e (avoided)}
$$

(Assuming Scope 2 = 30% of total.)

**Step 3 — Total Avoided Emissions:**

$$
\Delta CF_{total} = 604.8 + 151.2 = 756 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 4 — New Carbon Footprint:**

$$
CF_{new} = 4{,}200 - 756 = 3{,}444 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Reduction:**

$$
\eta_{reduction} = \dfrac{4{,}200 - 3{,}444}{4{,}200} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{reduction} = 18.0\%}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Reduction Strategies

| Strategy | Scope Affected | Typical Reduction | Capex (Indicative) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Biomass boiler (replace FO) | 1 | 50–70% of boiler emissions | High |
| Solar PV rooftop | 2 | 10–25% of grid | Medium |
| Heat recovery from exhaust | 1, 2 | 5–15% | Medium |
| Refrigerant retrofit (NH₃/CO₂) | 1 | 80–90% of refrigerant CF | High |
| LED + VFD retrofit | 2 | 8–15% | Low |
| Packaging lightweighting | 3 | 5–12% | Low |
| Local sourcing of raw materials | 3 | 10–20% transport CF | Nil |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Carbon Footprint Assessment Workflow (Cradle-to-Gate)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Goal and Scope Definition] --> B[Step2: System Boundary Setting]
    B --> C[Step3: Functional Unit Selection]
    C --> D[Step4: Life Cycle Inventory Data Collection]
    D --> E[Step5: Emission Factor Mapping]
    E --> F[Step6: GHG Calculation CO2e]
    F --> G[Step7: Hotspot Identification]
    G --> H[Step8: Interpretation and Reporting]
    H --> I[Step9: Reduction Strategy]
    I --> J[Step10: Verification by Third Party]
    J --> K[Certified CF Label and Disclosure]
```

### 4.2 Scope-Wise Emission Sources in Food Processing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Scope1 Direct]
        S1A[Boiler Fuel Combustion]
        S1B[Diesel Forklift and DG]
        S1C[Refrigerant Leakage]
        S1D[Onsite Wastewater CH4]
    end
    subgraph S2[Scope2 Indirect Energy]
        S2A[Purchased Grid Electricity]
        S2B[Purchased Steam]
    end
    subgraph S3[Scope3 Value Chain]
        S3A[Agricultural Production]
        S3B[Packaging Manufacturing]
        S3C[Inbound and Outbound Transport]
        S3D[Retail Cold Chain]
        S3E[End of Life Disposal]
    end
    S1 --> SUM[Total Carbon Footprint CO2e]
    S2 --> SUM
    S3 --> SUM
```

### 4.3 Carbon Footprint Reduction Strategy Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Food Plant Carbon Footprint] --> L1A[Energy Efficiency]
    ROOT --> L1B[Fuel Switching]
    ROOT --> L1C[Renewable Energy]
    ROOT --> L1D[Process Optimization]
    ROOT --> L1E[Supply Chain]
    L1A --> L2A1[VFD on Motors]
    L1A --> L2A2[Heat Recovery]
    L1A --> L2A3[LED Lighting]
    L1B --> L2B1[FO to PNG]
    L1B --> L2B2[Coal to Biomass]
    L1C --> L2C1[Solar PV]
    L1C --> L2C2[Wind Power]
    L1D --> L2D1[Heat Integration]
    L1D --> L2D2[Refrigerant Retrofit]
    L1E --> L2E1[Local Sourcing]
    L1E --> L2E2[Lightweight Packaging]
    L1E --> L2E3[Modal Shift Rail]
```

### 4.4 Hotspot Identification Matrix (Pareto Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Raw Milk Production: 55 percent] --> TOTAL
    H2[Plant Electricity: 18 percent] --> TOTAL
    H3[Packaging Materials: 12 percent] --> TOTAL
    H4[Boiler Fuel: 8 percent] --> TOTAL
    H5[Transport: 4 percent] --> TOTAL
    H6[Refrigerants: 2 percent] --> TOTAL
    H7[Wastewater: 1 percent] --> TOTAL
    TOTAL[Total Plant CF: 100 percent CO2e]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pareto Principle in CF:** Typically, **3–4 emission sources contribute >80%** of a food plant's total carbon footprint. Identifying these hotspots is the **first deliverable** of any CF assessment.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]**
**Define Carbon Footprint. List any four greenhouse gases with their GWP values.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Carbon Footprint is the total greenhouse gas emissions caused directly and indirectly by a food product, expressed in **kg CO₂ equivalent (CO₂e)** per functional unit (KTU: 1 Mark).

Four GHGs with GWP (100-year, IPCC AR5) (KTU: 2 Marks for any four):

| GHG | GWP-100 |
|:---:|:---:|
| CO₂ | 1 |
| CH₄ | 28 |
| N₂O | 265 |
| HFC-134a | 1,300 |
| SF₆ | 23,500 |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**
**Explain the three scopes of GHG emissions with two examples each from a food processing plant.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Greenhouse Gas Protocol** classifies emissions into three scopes (KTU: 1 Mark definition):

- **Scope 1 (Direct):** Owned sources. Examples — natural gas in boilers, refrigerant leaks from cold storage (KTU: 0.67 Mark).
- **Scope 2 (Indirect Energy):** Purchased electricity/steam. Examples — grid electricity for motors, purchased steam from cogeneration plant (KTU: 0.67 Mark).
- **Scope 3 (Value Chain):** Upstream + downstream indirect. Examples — agricultural fertilizer use, outbound truck transport of finished goods (KTU: 0.66 Mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step methodology** for assessing the carbon footprint of a packaged fruit juice using **ISO 14067 framework**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A beverage plant consumes **150,000 m³ of natural gas/year** and **2.4 million kWh/year** of grid electricity. Compute the **annual Scope 1 + Scope 2 carbon footprint**. Also find the **per-litre CF** if annual production is **20 million litres**. Use EF: PNG = 2.05 kg CO₂e/m³; Grid = 0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

ISO 14067 methodology for packaged fruit juice (Cradle-to-Gate):

1. **Goal Definition** (KTU: 1 Mark): Quantify CFP per 1 litre of packaged juice.

2. **Scope Definition** (KTU: 1 Mark): Cradle-to-gate boundary — fruit cultivation → juice processing → packaging → factory gate.

3. **Life Cycle Inventory (LCI)** (KTU: 2 Marks): Data on:
   - Agricultural inputs (fertilizers, pesticides, water for orchards).
   - Energy in plant (steam, electricity, refrigeration).
   - Packaging materials (PET bottles, caps, labels, shrink-wrap).
   - Wastewater & solid waste generated.

4. **Life Cycle Impact Assessment (LCIA)** (KTU: 1 Mark): Convert each GHG to CO₂e using emission factors and GWP.

5. **Interpretation** (KTU: 1 Mark): Identify hotspots (e.g., orange cultivation = 40%, PET packaging = 25%, processing electricity = 20%, transport = 15%).

6. **Reporting & Critical Review** (KTU: 1 Mark): Document per ISO 14067 reporting format; undergo third-party verification for credibility.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Scope 1 (Natural Gas Combustion):**

$$
CF_1 = V_{NG} \times EF_{NG} = 150{,}000 \times 2.05 = 307{,}500 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

(KTU: 2 Marks)

**Step 2 — Scope 2 (Grid Electricity):**

$$
CF_2 = E_{grid} \times EF_{grid} = 2{,}400{,}000 \times 0.716 = 1{,}718{,}400 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

(KTU: 2 Marks)

**Step 3 — Total CF (Scope 1 + 2):**

$$
CF_{total} = 307{,}500 + 1{,}718{,}400 = 2{,}025{,}900 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e / year}
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

**Step 4 — Per-Litre CF:**

$$
CF_{per\_litre} = \dfrac{2{,}025{,}900}{20{,}000{,}000} = 0.1013 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e / litre}
$$

(KTU: 2 Marks for final answer with units)

$$
\boxed{CF_{per\_litre} \approx 0.101 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e / litre}}
$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss the **major sources of carbon emissions** in a typical food processing plant. Categorize them under Scope 1, 2, and 3 with specific examples. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A frozen seafood plant has the following annual data: Refrigerant R-404a leak = **25 kg/year**; Diesel = **20,000 L/year** (forklifts); Furnace oil = **100,000 L/year**; Grid electricity = **800,000 kWh/year**. Calculate the **total CF** and the **percentage contribution of refrigerants** to Scope 1 emissions. Use: GWP(R-404a) = 3,922; EF(diesel) = 2.68 kg CO₂e/L; EF(FO) = 3.20 kg CO₂e/L; EF(grid) = 0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Major Carbon Emission Sources in Food Processing:**

**1. Scope 1 — Direct Emissions (KTU: 2 Marks):**
- **Steam boilers**: combustion of natural gas, furnace oil, coal, biomass.
- **Diesel generators and forklifts**: backup power, material handling.
- **Refrigerant leakage** from cold storage — high GWP gases (HFC-134a, R-404a) have very high CF impact.
- **On-site wastewater treatment**: methane from anaerobic digestion.
- **Process emissions**: e.g., CO₂ from fermentation in breweries.

**2. Scope 2 — Indirect from Purchased Energy (KTU: 2 Marks):**
- **Grid electricity** for motors, pumps, compressors, lighting, refrigeration.
- **Purchased steam** from cogeneration/CHP plants.

**3. Scope 3 — Value Chain Emissions (KTU: 2 Marks):**
- **Agriculture**: fertilizer manufacture (N₂O), livestock enteric CH₄, paddy CH₄.
- **Packaging**: PET, aluminium, paperboard — virgin material production.
- **Transport**: inbound raw materials + outbound finished goods (truck, rail, ship).
- **Retail cold chain**: refrigerated display at retail outlets.
- **End-of-life**: landfill CH₄, recycling energy, incineration CO₂.

**Conclusion (KTU: 1 Mark):** Scope 3 typically dominates (60–80%) but is hardest to control; Scope 2 is the most actionable via energy efficiency and renewable energy procurement.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Refrigerant R-404a Emissions:**

$$
CF_{ref} = 25 \times 3{,}922 = 98{,}050 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

**Step 2 — Diesel Emissions:**

$$
CF_{diesel} = 20{,}000 \times 2.68 = 53{,}600 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

**Step 3 — Furnace Oil Emissions:**

$$
CF_{FO} = 100{,}000 \times 3.20 = 320{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

**Step 4 — Total Scope 1:**

$$
CF_1 = 98{,}050 + 53{,}600 + 320{,}000 = 471{,}650 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

**Step 5 — Scope 2 (Grid Electricity):**

$$
CF_2 = 800{,}000 \times 0.716 = 572{,}800 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e}
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

**Step 6 — Total CF:**

$$
CF_{total} = 471{,}650 + 572{,}800 = 1{,}044{,}450 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e / year}
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

**Step 7 — Refrigerant % of Scope 1:**

$$
\%_{ref} = \dfrac{98{,}050}{471{,}650} \times 100 = 20.79\%
$$

(KTU: 1 Mark)

$$
\boxed{CF_{total} \approx 1{,}044.45 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e / year}; \text{ Refrigerants} = 20.79\% \text{ of Scope 1}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by GWP for refrigerants and CH₄/N₂O** — losing 2–3 marks. Always convert to CO₂e.
> 2. **Mixing Scope 1 and Scope 2** — these MUST be reported separately per GHG Protocol. Combining them = –2 marks.
> 3. **Using wrong emission factor units** — e.g., using kg CO₂e/kWh instead of kg CO₂e/MWh, or vice versa. Always check.
> 4. **Not stating the standard (AR4 vs. AR5)** when quoting GWP values. Examiner deducts 0.5–1 mark.
> 5. **Final answer without units** (e.g., writing "0.085" instead of "0.085 kg CO₂e/litre") = –1 mark guaranteed.
> 6. **Forgetting to specify the system boundary** (cradle-to-gate vs. gate-to-gate). Always state it in methodology.
> 7. **Not showing the formula before substituting values** — KTU wants the formula explicitly written.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**

- ✅ **Carbon Footprint Formula:** $CF = \sum (AD_i \times EF_i \times GWP_i)$
- ✅ **Three Scopes:** Scope 1 (direct), Scope 2 (purchased energy), Scope 3 (value chain).
- ✅ **GWP-100 Values (AR5):** CO₂ = 1; CH₄ = 28; N₂O = 265; HFC-134a = 1,300; R-404a = 3,922; SF₆ = 23,500.
- ✅ **Key Standards:** ISO 14064 (organization), ISO 14067 (product), PAS 2050, GHG Protocol.
- ✅ **India Grid Emission Factor (CEA 2022):** 0.716 kg CO₂e/kWh.
- ✅ **Common Fuel EFs:** PNG = 2.05 kg CO₂e/m³; Furnace oil = 3.20 kg CO₂e/L; Diesel = 2.68 kg CO₂e/L; Coal = 2.42 kg CO₂e/kg.
- ✅ **Pareto Principle:** ~80% CF from ~20% sources — always identify hotspots first.
- ✅ **Per-Unit Reporting:** Always normalize to functional unit (kg, litre, tonne-km).
- ✅ **Reduction Order:** Energy efficiency → Fuel switching → Renewables → Process integration → Supply chain → Offsets.
- ✅ **Food Industry Specific Hotspots:** Refrigerants (high GWP!), agricultural upstream, packaging, transport.
- ✅ **System Boundaries:** Cradle-to-gate, cradle-to-grave, gate-to-gate — MUST be explicitly stated.
- ✅ **Always include units** in final answers — KTU is strict on this.
- ✅ **Biomass CO₂ is climate-neutral** under accounting rules but CH₄ from incomplete combustion is NOT.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strategies for Mitigating Greenhouse Gas Emissions Through Energy Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INSUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Strategies for Mitigating Greenhouse Gas Emissions Through Energy Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions** are gaseous constituents of the atmosphere, both natural and anthropogenic, that absorb and re-emit infrared radiation in the wavelength range corresponding to thermal emission from the Earth's surface. Under the **Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC)** and the **UNFCCC Kyoto Protocol**, six gases are regulated: **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)**, **Methane (CH₄)**, **Nitrous Oxide (N₂O)**, **Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)**, **Perfluorocarbons (PFCs)**, and **Sulphur Hexafluoride (SF₆)**.

**Energy Efficiency** in the food industry is defined as the ratio of useful energy output (productive food-processing work, thermal energy utilized, refrigeration cooling delivered) to the total energy input (fuel, electricity, steam) — expressed as a dimensionless ratio, percentage, or specific energy consumption (SEC) in **MJ per tonne of product**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (Module 4):** Mitigation strategies in the food industry are classified into four hierarchical tiers: (i) Energy Avoidance, (ii) Energy Efficiency, (iii) Energy Recovery (Heat/Steam Integration), and (iv) Fuel Substitution with Renewables. The food sector alone accounts for approximately **30%** of global energy consumption and nearly **22%** of total anthropogenic GHG emissions (FAO, *Food wastage footprint*).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your household kitchen. When you boil 5 litres of water in an open vessel, a lot of steam escapes uselessly into the air — that wasted energy went up the chimney of your "kitchen factory." Now place a tight lid on the pot and use a steam pipe to warm the dish-washing water next door. **You have just converted waste energy into useful energy** — this is the *spirit* of energy efficiency in a food plant.

In a commercial bakery, refrigeration, ovens, and steam boilers all dump heat into the atmosphere. By installing a **heat exchanger** on the refrigeration condensers, that rejected heat can pre-warm boiler feedwater — saving fuel, reducing CO₂, and lowering operating cost simultaneously. The food plant becomes a *closed energy loop*, much like a recirculating aquaponics pond where fish waste fertilizes plants and plants filter water for fish.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundaries

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Food-Industry Value |
|---|---|---|
| Global Warming Potential (100 yr) | GWP₁₀₀ | **CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265** |
| Specific Energy Consumption | SEC | **2 – 8 GJ/tonne** of finished product |
| Energy Efficiency Ratio | EER | **2.5 – 4.0** (refrigeration) |
| Carbon Intensity | CI | **0.4 – 1.2 tCO₂/tonne** product |

> [!NOTE]
> **GWP** is a relative measure of how much heat a GHG traps compared to CO₂. 1 kg of CH₄ traps the same heat as **28 kg of CO₂** over 100 years. Methane is critical in food processing because **anaerobic digestion of organic waste** and **enteric fermentation** are the dominant non-CO₂ GHG sources.

### 1.4 Sources of GHG in a Food Plant

1. **Scope 1 (Direct):** Boiler fuel combustion (CO₂, CH₄), refrigeration leaks (HFCs), wastewater lagoons (CH₄).
2. **Scope 2 (Indirect):** Purchased grid electricity (CO₂ in coal/gas-heavy grids like Kerala's NTPC mix).
3. **Scope 3 (Value-chain):** Raw-material farming (N₂O from fertilizers), packaging, transport, retail cold chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Expected Definition:** *"Energy efficiency is the most cost-effective, near-term strategy for GHG mitigation because every kWh saved avoids ~0.82 kg CO₂ in the Indian grid average emission factor (CEA, 2023)."* Memorize this ratio.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Tier Mitigation Framework

The strategic hierarchy for a food plant manager follows a **descending cost-effectiveness curve** (most savings per rupee invested at the top):

**Tier 1 — Energy Avoidance & Demand Reduction**
- Switching to LED lighting (saves 60–80% vs incandescent/HID)
- Variable Frequency Drives (VFD) on pumps and fans
- Insulation upgrades on steam pipes, ovens, cold rooms
- Production scheduling to avoid peak-tariff hours
- Cold-room door curtains, strip curtains, automatic doors

**Tier 2 — Energy Efficiency in Process Equipment**
- High-efficiency motors (IE3/IE4) replacing IE1/IE2
- Refrigeration: floating head pressure, electronic expansion valves, sub-cooling
- Steam systems: blowdown heat recovery, condensate return, leak management
- Baking: hot-oil vs direct-fired comparison
- Compressed air: leak reduction (typical 20–30% leakage), pressure optimization
- Drying: heat-pump dryers, multi-effect evaporators, mechanical vapour recompression (MVR)

**Tier 3 — Energy Recovery & Process Integration**
- **Pinch Analysis** (ΔT_min optimization, typically 5–20 °C in food plants)
- Heat recovery from refrigeration condensers → boiler feedwater
- Combined Heat and Power (CHP) / Cogeneration
- Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC) on exhaust streams
- Anaerobic digestion of food waste → biogas (CHP) → electricity + heat

**Tier 4 — Fuel Substitution & Renewables**
- Biomass boilers (rice husk, bagasse, coconut shell — Kerala-specific)
- Solar thermal for pre-heating boiler feedwater
- Solar PV for grid-supplied loads
- Hydrogen / biofuel pilot trials
- Geothermal / solar cooling for cold storage

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Tier

- **Why Tier 1 first?** A saved kWh costs roughly **₹2–3** (insulation, controls), whereas generating a kWh from a solar panel costs **₹4–5** and from coal is **₹6–8** to society through pollution. The cheapest energy is the one you do not consume.
- **How Pinch Analysis works:** Construct composite curves of hot and cold streams; the point of closest approach defines the **Minimum Approach Temperature (ΔT_min)**. Above this pinch, no cooling is needed; below, no heating is needed. Set the **Mercury / Utility Target** for minimum hot and cold utility.
- **How CHP works:** A single fuel input (natural gas/biogas) drives a prime mover (gas engine/turbine) producing electricity; waste heat from jacket cooling and exhaust is recovered as hot water or low-pressure steam — overall efficiency 70–85% vs 30–40% for separate heat & power.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Application / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CO₂ from fuel combustion | $E_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times NCV \times EF \times \frac{44}{12}$ | kg CO₂ | $m_{fuel}$ = fuel mass (kg), $NCV$ = Net Calorific Value (GJ/kg), $EF$ = Emission Factor (tC/TJ) |
| 2 | CO₂ from grid electricity | $E_{CO_2} = E_{grid} \times 0.82$ | kg CO₂ | Indian CEA grid factor 2023 = **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** |
| 3 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \frac{E_{input}}{M_{product}}$ | MJ/tonne | Used for benchmarking dairy, sugar, bakery |
| 4 | Energy Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{E_{useful}}{E_{input}} \times 100$ | % | Boiler η = 60–90%, motor η = 85–96% |
| 5 | Coefficient of Performance (COP) | $COP = \frac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{compresssor}}$ | — | Refrigeration COP = 2–5; heat-pump COP = 3–6 |
| 6 | Payback Period | $PB = \frac{C_{investment}}{\Delta C_{annual}}$ | years | Simple PB; use NPV/IRR for major projects |
| 7 | Pinch ΔT_min | $T_{hot,min} - T_{cold,max} = \Delta T_{min}$ | °C | Food plants: 5–20 °C |
| 8 | CHP Efficiency | $\eta_{CHP} = \frac{W_{elec} + Q_{heat}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | % | Range 70–85% |
| 9 | CO₂-equivalent emission | $E_{CO_2e} = \sum_i (m_i \times GWP_i)$ | kg CO₂e | Methane × 28, N₂O × 265 |
| 10 | Annual Energy Cost Saving | $AES = E_{saved} \times Rate$ | ₹/year | Multiply kWh saved by ₹/kWh tariff |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion:** $1 \text{ toe} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} = 11{,}630 \text{ kWh}$ (tonne of oil equivalent). Often used in BEE-Star labelling audits.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations underpin the **Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) Scheme** of India's Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), which mandates Specific Energy Consumption reduction targets for **Designated Consumers (DCs)** in the food sector — including dairy, sugar, fertilizer (related), and pulp & paper. The ISO 50001 Energy Management System standard is a globally accepted implementation tool, and auditors verify SEC, M&V (Measurement & Verification) per IPMVP protocols.

A Pinch Analysis study at a typical **Kerala dairy plant** (capacity 5 lakh L/day) can identify **2.5–4.0 GJ/hour** of recoverable heat, translating to **₹1.5–2 crore/year** fuel savings with a payback of 14–22 months.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations: Core Energy & Emission Equations

#### 3.1.1 Derivation of CO₂ from Fuel Combustion

Start with the **stoichiometric combustion** of a generic hydrocarbon fuel $C_x H_y$:

$$
C_x H_y + \left( x + \frac{y}{4} \right) O_2 \rightarrow x \, CO_2 + \frac{y}{2} \, H_2 O
$$

The mass of carbon in the fuel is given by the carbon content $C_{frac}$ (kg C per kg fuel). The mass of CO₂ produced per kg of fuel is obtained by multiplying the carbon mass by the ratio of molecular weights:

$$
\frac{M_{CO_2}}{M_C} = \frac{44 \text{ kg/kmol}}{12 \text{ kg/kmol}}
$$

This yields the master emission formula:

$$
E_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times C_{frac} \times \frac{44}{12} \times \frac{1000}{1}
$$

where $E_{CO_2}$ is in **kg CO₂**, $m_{fuel}$ is in **tonnes**, and $C_{frac}$ is the mass fraction of carbon in the fuel (e.g., 0.55–0.65 for coal, 0.75 for diesel, 0.85 for natural gas).

#### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Payback Period for an Energy Project

Let the investment cost be $C_{inv}$ (₹) and the annual monetary savings be $S$ (₹/year). The cumulative cash flow as a function of time $t$ is:

$$
CF(t) = -C_{inv} + S \cdot t
$$

The payback period is the value of $t$ at which $CF(t) = 0$:

$$
0 = -C_{inv} + S \cdot PB
$$

Solving explicitly:

$$
PB = \frac{C_{inv}}{S}
$$

This is the **Simple Payback Period (SPP)**. For more rigorous analysis, **Net Present Value (NPV)** is preferred:

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{S_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_{inv}
$$

where $r$ is the discount rate (typically 10–12% in Indian industries) and $n$ is the project life (10–15 years for energy projects).

#### 3.1.3 Derivation of Pinch-Analysis Minimum Utility Targets

Consider a food process with $N_h$ hot streams and $N_c$ cold streams. For each hot stream $i$ with mass flow $\dot{m}_{h,i}$ and specific heat $C_{p,h,i}$, the heat release is:

$$
Q_{h,i} = \dot{m}_{h,i} \cdot C_{p,h,i} \cdot \Delta T_{h,i}
$$

Similarly for cold streams. Construct a **Problem Table** by shifting cold-stream temperatures upward by $\Delta T_{min}/2$ and hot-stream temperatures downward by $\Delta T_{min}/2$ on the temperature axis. The minimum hot utility $Q_{H,min}$ is:

$$
Q_{H,min} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_h} Q_{h,i} - Q_{recoverable,max}
$$

where $Q_{recoverable,max}$ is the maximum enthalpy that can be transferred from hot to cold streams subject to the pinch temperature constraint.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Boiler Energy Audit

**Problem Statement:** A rice-milling food plant operates a 6 tonne/h fire-tube boiler running 18 hours/day, 300 days/year on furnace oil. Stack gas temperature is 320 °C (excess air 18%). Estimate the annual CO₂ emission, energy loss, and potential saving from installing an economizer that drops stack temperature to 180 °C. Furnace oil: NCV = 40.4 MJ/kg, $C_{frac} = 0.85$.

**Step 1 — Fuel consumption per year:**

$$
m_{fuel} = 6{,}000 \text{ kg/h} \times 18 \text{ h/day} \times 300 \text{ days/year}
$$

$$
m_{fuel} = 32{,}400{,}000 \text{ kg/year} = 32{,}400 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual CO₂ emission (use the master formula):**

$$
E_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times C_{frac} \times \frac{44}{12}
$$

$$
E_{CO_2} = 32{,}400 \times 0.85 \times \frac{44}{12}
$$

$$
E_{CO_2} = 32{,}400 \times 0.85 \times 3.667
$$

$$
E_{CO_2} = 32{,}400 \times 3.117
$$

$$
E_{CO_2} \approx 100{,}980 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

**Step 3 — Stack heat loss at 320 °C (rough estimate, 22% of fuel energy):**

$$
Q_{stack,old} = 0.22 \times m_{fuel} \times NCV
$$

$$
Q_{stack,old} = 0.22 \times 32{,}400 \times 40.4 \text{ MJ}
$$

$$
Q_{stack,old} \approx 287{,}950 \text{ GJ/year}
$$

**Step 4 — After economizer (stack at 180 °C, loss drops to ~12%):**

$$
Q_{stack,new} = 0.12 \times 32{,}400 \times 40.4 \text{ MJ}
$$

$$
Q_{stack,new} \approx 157{,}065 \text{ GJ/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Energy saved:**

$$
\Delta Q = Q_{stack,old} - Q_{stack,new} = 287{,}950 - 157{,}065 = 130{,}885 \text{ GJ/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Fuel saved and CO₂ avoided:**

$$
\Delta m_{fuel} = \frac{\Delta Q}{NCV} = \frac{130{,}885}{40.4} \approx 3{,}240 \text{ tonnes fuel/year}
$$

$$
\Delta E_{CO_2} = 3{,}240 \times 0.85 \times 3.667 \approx 10{,}098 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

**Step 7 — Monetary saving (furnace oil @ ₹80/kg):**

$$
S = 3{,}240{,}000 \text{ kg} \times ₹80/\text{kg} = ₹25.92 \text{ crore/year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A ₹25 lakh economizer investment at this scale yields **₹25+ crore/year** savings and prevents **10,000 tonnes CO₂/year** — payback under 2 weeks. This is why Tier-3 energy recovery is a board-exam favourite.

### 3.3 Comparative Strategy Matrix

| Strategy | Typical SEC Reduction | CO₂ Avoided | Payback | Capital Intensity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LED Lighting | 1–2% | Low | < 1 yr | Low |
| VFD on pumps | 20–50% | Medium | 1–3 yr | Medium |
| Pinch Heat Integration | 15–35% | High | 1–3 yr | Medium-High |
| CHP / Cogeneration | 25–40% | High | 3–6 yr | High |
| Anaerobic Digestion | 60–90% (waste) | Very High | 4–7 yr | High |
| Solar PV | 25–100% (electrical) | Medium | 4–7 yr | Medium-High |
| Heat-Pump Dryer | 50–70% | High | 2–4 yr | Medium |

### 3.4 Analytical Calculation: COP Improvement in a Cold Storage

A 200-truck-load cold storage for frozen fish runs at COP = 2.1. Upgrading to a **VFD-controlled screw compressor with floating head pressure and sub-cooler** improves COP to 3.4. Compressor load = 120 kW running 20 h/day, 350 days/year. Tariff = ₹9.50/kWh.

**Old electricity consumption:**

$$
E_{old} = \frac{120 \times 20 \times 350}{2.1} = 400{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**New electricity consumption:**

$$
E_{new} = \frac{120 \times 20 \times 350}{3.4} = 247{,}058 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Savings:**

$$
\Delta E = 400{,}000 - 247{,}058 = 152{,}942 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

$$
S = 152{,}942 \times 9.50 = ₹14.53 \text{ lakh/year}
$$

**CO₂ avoided:**

$$
\Delta E_{CO_2} = 152{,}942 \times 0.82 = 125{,}412 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

This single retrofit — typical cost ₹35 lakh — yields payback ≈ **2.4 years** and avoids **125 tonnes CO₂/year**, equivalent to taking **~27 cars** off Kerala's roads annually.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Pinch Point** is the most thermally constrained location in a process. Heat must NOT be transferred across the pinch; doing so increases net utility demand. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Define Greenhouse Gases. List the six gases regulated under the Kyoto Protocol along with their 100-year Global Warming Potentials.**

**Model Answer:**
Greenhouse Gases are atmospheric gases that absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, causing global warming. The six gases regulated under the **Kyoto Protocol** are:

| Gas | GWP₁₀₀ |
|---|---|
| Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) | **1** |
| Methane (CH₄) | **28** |
| Nitrous Oxide (N₂O) | **265** |
| Hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs) | **130 – 1,300** |
| Perfluorocarbons (PFCs) | **6,630 – 11,100** |
| Sulphur Hexafluoride (SF₆) | **23,500** |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for table)*

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**Explain the concept of Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and state typical values for a food processing plant.**

**Model Answer:**
SEC is the energy input per unit of finished product, expressed as **MJ/tonne** or **kWh/tonne**. It is the key benchmarking metric under the **BEE-PAT scheme** in India.

$$
SEC = \frac{E_{input}}{M_{product}}
$$

**Typical values:**
- Dairy processing: 2.0 – 4.0 GJ/tonne
- Sugar mills: 3.0 – 6.0 GJ/tonne
- Edible oil refining: 1.5 – 3.0 GJ/tonne
- Bakery: 4.0 – 8.0 GJ/tonne

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for definition, 1 for typical values)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Process Integration Focus

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Pinch Analysis** methodology. Define the terms **Pinch Point**, **ΔT_min**, **Minimum Hot Utility** and **Minimum Cold Utility**. State typical ΔT_min values for the food industry.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A food processing plant has the following streams. Construct a grid diagram and identify the pinch point.

| Stream | Type | Supply T (°C) | Target T (°C) | CP (kW/°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | Hot | 180 | 80 | 3.0 |
| H2 | Hot | 150 | 60 | 2.0 |
| C1 | Cold | 60 | 140 | 2.5 |
| C2 | Cold | 30 | 110 | 1.5 |

Take ΔT_min = 20 °C. Compute minimum hot and cold utility targets.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pinch Analysis Methodology** *(7 marks)*

Pinch Analysis is a thermodynamic methodology developed by **Linnhoff** for optimizing heat recovery in a process plant. The steps are:

1. **Data Extraction:** Identify all process streams with supply/target temperatures and heat-capacity flow rates (CP).
2. **Shifted Temperature Axis:** Hot streams are shifted downward by ΔT_min/2, cold streams upward by ΔT_min/2.
3. **Composite Curves:** Combine all hot streams into one hot composite curve and all cold streams into one cold composite curve. The horizontal overlap shows **recoverable heat**; the horizontal gap shows **utility demand**.
4. **Pinch Point:** Point of minimum approach between the two curves; defines ΔT_min.
5. **Energy Targeting:** Read minimum hot utility $Q_{H,min}$ (above pinch) and minimum cold utility $Q_{C,min}$ (below pinch).
6. **HEN Design:** Place heat exchangers above and below the pinch; **never transfer heat across the pinch**.

Typical food industry ΔT_min = **5 – 20 °C** (lower than petrochemical due to milder temperatures and process flexibility).

**Diagram (Grid Diagram Sketch — verbal description for the answer sheet):**
```
   Hot Streams  →  →  →  →  |  ←  ←  ←  ←  Cold Streams
       H1 ════════════════════╗
                                 ║══ C1
       H2 ═══════════════╗        ║
                            ║══════╝══ C2
                          PINCH
```
*Above pinch:* no cooling utility; *Below pinch:* no heating utility.

**(b) Numerical Solution** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Shift temperatures** by ΔT_min/2 = 10 °C:

| Stream | $T_{supply}^{shifted}$ (°C) | $T_{target}^{shifted}$ (°C) | CP (kW/°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| H1 | 170 | 70 | 3.0 |
| H2 | 140 | 50 | 2.0 |
| C1 | 70 | 150 | 2.5 |
| C2 | 40 | 120 | 1.5 |

**Step 2 — Enthalpy on the shifted grid:**

- H1: $\Delta H_{H1} = 3.0 \times (170 - 70) = 300 \text{ kW}$
- H2: $\Delta H_{H2} = 2.0 \times (140 - 50) = 180 \text{ kW}$
- C1: $\Delta H_{C1} = 2.5 \times (150 - 70) = 200 \text{ kW}$
- C2: $\Delta H_{C2} = 1.5 \times (120 - 40) = 120 \text{ kW}$

**Step 3 — Problem Table (interval analysis)**

| Interval (°C) | Net CP (kW/°C) | ΔH (kW) | Cumulative Q |
|---|---|---|---|
| 170 – 150 | 3.0 (H1) | -60 | -60 |
| 150 – 140 | 3.0 - 2.5 = 0.5 | -5 | -65 |
| 140 – 120 | 0.5 - 2.0 = -1.5 | -30 | -95 |
| 120 – 70 | -1.5 - 1.5 = -3.0 | -150 | -245 |
| 70 – 50 | -3.0 + 0 = -3.0 | -60 | -305 |
| 50 – 40 | -3.0 | -30 | -335 |

**Step 4 — Locate pinch:** The interval where the cumulative enthalpy changes sign. Adding from the top: at 170 °C, Q = 0. The most-negative cumulative value is **-335 kW** at 40 °C. The minimum heating utility is the maximum negative excursion: $Q_{H,min} = 335 \text{ kW}$.

**Pinch point** lies in the **50 – 40 °C interval** (i.e., shifted pinch) → true hot pinch ≈ **50 °C** and true cold pinch ≈ **70 °C** (ΔT_min = 20 °C).

**Step 5 — Cold utility:**

$$
Q_{C,min} = Q_{H,min} + \sum \Delta H_{cold} - \sum \Delta H_{hot} = 335 + (200 + 120) - (300 + 180)
$$

$$
Q_{C,min} = 335 + 320 - 480 = 175 \text{ kW}
$$

**Final answer:**
- $Q_{H,min} = 335 \text{ kW}$, $Q_{C,min} = 175 \text{ kW}$
- Hot Pinch: 50 °C, Cold Pinch: 70 °C

*(Full marks require both the data extraction step and the problem-table procedure.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — CHP and Biogas Focus

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the principle of **Combined Heat and Power (CHP)**. With a block diagram, explain its application in a food processing plant. List the key performance indicators used to evaluate CHP.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A dairy plant generates 25 tonnes/day of organic waste (COD = 50,000 mg/L, biodegradable). A proposed anaerobic digester will produce biogas with 60% methane. The CHP engine has electrical efficiency 35% and thermal efficiency 50%. Calculate (i) daily biogas volume, (ii) daily electricity generation, (iii) daily heat recovery, and (iv) annual CO₂ avoided. Take calorific value of methane = 55.5 MJ/kg, density 0.72 kg/m³, grid factor 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle of CHP** *(7 marks)*

Combined Heat and Power (Cogeneration) is the simultaneous generation of **electrical energy and useful thermal energy** from a single fuel input. Conventional systems have separate generation with combined efficiency of 30–45% (significant losses in transmission and condenser). In CHP:

- **Prime mover** (gas engine, gas turbine, steam turbine, or fuel cell) drives an electricity generator.
- **Waste heat** from the jacket cooling, lube-oil system, and exhaust gas is captured and used for process heating, hot water, or steam.

**Block Diagram (verbal):**

```
   FUEL ──→ [PRIME MOVER] ──→ ELECTRICITY
              │   │   │
              ↓   ↓   ↓
        Jacket Heat → [HEAT RECOVERY BOILER] → HOT WATER / STEAM
        Lube Oil Heat ─┘
        Exhaust Gas  ─┘
```

**KPIs for CHP evaluation:**
1. **Overall energy efficiency** $\eta_{CHP} = (W_{elec} + Q_{heat})/Q_{fuel}$ — target **> 70%**.
2. **Power-to-Heat ratio** (P/Q) — must match the plant's demand profile.
3. **Capacity utilisation factor** — hours of operation vs available hours.
4. **Fuel utilization efficiency** (FUE).
5. **Heat-to-power ratio** flexibility.
6. **Payback period** and **NPV** at a given tariff and fuel cost.

**(b) Numerical Solution** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Daily biogas volume (assuming 0.5 m³ biogas/kg COD removed, typical for high-rate digesters):**

$$
V_{biogas} = 25{,}000 \text{ kg/day} \times 0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}
$$

$$
V_{biogas} = 12{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

**Step 2 — Methane mass per day:**

$$
m_{CH_4} = 12{,}500 \times 0.60 \times 0.72 = 5{,}400 \text{ kg CH}_4/\text{day}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily electricity generated (at 35% η_elec):**

$$
W_{elec} = 5{,}400 \times 55.5 \times 0.35 = 104{,}895 \text{ MJ/day}
$$

Converting to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):

$$
W_{elec} = \frac{104{,}895}{3.6} = 29{,}137 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Daily heat recovery (at 50% η_thermal):**

$$
Q_{heat} = 5{,}400 \times 55.5 \times 0.50 = 149{,}850 \text{ MJ/day} = 41{,}625 \text{ kWh/day (thermal)}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual CO₂ avoided (electricity displaced from grid, biogas CO₂ is biogenic and considered carbon-neutral):**

$$
\Delta E_{CO_2} = 29{,}137 \times 365 \times 0.82 = 8{,}720{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

$$
\Delta E_{CO_2} \approx 8{,}720 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

**Final answer summary:**
- Biogas = **12,500 m³/day**
- Electricity = **29,137 kWh/day**
- Heat = **41,625 kWh/day (thermal)**
- CO₂ avoided = **8,720 tonnes CO₂/year**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit conversion mistakes:** The most common error is forgetting to convert MJ to kWh (divide by 3.6). KTU examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Confusing mass and volume of methane:** $1 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 = 0.72 \text{ kg}$ at STP. Students often wrongly use density of air.
> 3. **Biogenic CO₂ accounting:** Methane from food waste / biomass is conventionally counted as **carbon-neutral** in CO₂ emission inventories (the carbon was recently fixed by photosynthesis). Do **not** add its combustion CO₂ to the "avoided" calculation — only the displaced grid electricity counts.
> 4. **Pinch Analysis transcription errors:** Skipping the temperature shift by ΔT_min/2 is a guaranteed **2-mark** loss.
> 5. **Using GWP of CH₄ in a CO₂e question but forgetting the time horizon** (20-year GWP is 84, 100-year is 28). State the horizon explicitly.
> 6. **Reading kWh = kW:** Energy is kWh, power is kW. Both appear in CHP and refrigeration problems.
> 7. **Not labelling the shifted vs actual temperature axis** in the Problem Table — KTU awards marks for clarity.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Final-Day Cram Sheet)**

- **Six Kyoto GHGs:** CO₂ (1), CH₄ (28), N₂O (265), HFCs, PFCs, SF₆. **Memorize the 100-year GWPs.**
- **CO₂ from fuel:** $E_{CO_2} = m_{fuel} \times C_{frac} \times 44/12$. Know the 44/12 carbon-to-CO₂ conversion.
- **Indian grid emission factor:** **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** (CEA 2023). This is a **mandatory** number for KTU.
- **1 toe = 41.868 GJ = 11,630 kWh.** Useful for unit conversion problems.
- **SEC = Energy input / Product output** (MJ/tonne). Lower SEC = better energy efficiency.
- **Pinch Analysis ΔT_min:** Food industry = 5–20 °C. Hot pinch above, cold pinch below. **No heat transfer across the pinch.**
- **CHP efficiency:** 70–85% combined (vs 30–40% separate). Always specify the prime mover type.
- **COP of refrigeration:** 2.0–4.0 (typical), heat-pump dryer 3.0–6.0.
- **Four mitigation tiers** (in order): Avoid → Efficiency → Recovery → Fuel Substitution. Always recommend the cheapest first.
- **BEE PAT scheme** + **ISO 50001 EnMS** are the regulatory frameworks.
- **Biogenic CO₂ is carbon-neutral**; only count displaced fossil emissions.
- **Anaerobic digestion** of food waste: typical yield **0.3–0.5 m³ biogas/kg COD**.
- **Always show the Problem Table** for Pinch questions — partial marks are awarded for the table even if the final answer is wrong.
- **State assumptions explicitly** (operating hours, fuel NCV, density of methane at STP) in every numerical answer.
- **Topical Kerala context:** Rice-husk boilers, coconut-shell gasifiers, fish-cold-storage CHP, dairy biogas plants, coir-pith biomass — these are high-yield, locally relevant examples KTU examiners love.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Strategies for Mitigating GHG Emissions Through Energy Efficiency**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies on Successful Implementation of Emission Reduction Initiatives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT752 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT IN FOOD INSUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies on Successful Implementation of Emission Reduction Initiatives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Emission Reduction Initiatives in the Food Industry** refer to a structured portfolio of operational, technological, energy-efficiency, renewable-energy, and supply-chain strategies deployed by food manufacturing enterprises to systematically lower their **Scope 1, Scope 2, and Scope 3** greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions as defined by the *Greenhouse Gas Protocol Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standard*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Formal Definitions (Syllabus Anchors):**
> - **Scope 1 Emissions:** Direct GHG emissions from sources owned or controlled by the company (boilers, furnaces, on-site vehicles, refrigeration leaks).
> - **Scope 2 Emissions:** Indirect GHG emissions from purchased electricity, steam, heat, or cooling consumed by the company.
> - **Scope 3 Emissions:** All other indirect emissions occurring in the value chain (upstream agriculture, packaging, transport, downstream retail, consumer use, end-of-life).
> - **Carbon Neutrality:** Achieving a state where net CO₂-equivalent emissions released into the atmosphere are zero, typically via a mix of reductions and verified offsets.
> - **Net-Zero:** A deeper commitment where residual emissions are matched by removals (not offsets alone), aligned with the *Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi)*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen Diet"**
>
> Imagine a large restaurant kitchen that produces three types of "mess":
> 1. **Smoke from its own stoves and ovens** (Scope 1 — direct on-site combustion).
> 2. **Steam leaks from the central heating pipes the landlord provides** (Scope 2 — bought energy).
> 3. **Food waste from customers' plates and packaging from suppliers** (Scope 3 — value chain).
>
> The restaurant cannot just clean its own stoves and ignore the rest. A *successful emission reduction initiative* is like a holistic "kitchen diet": it installs induction stoves (electrification), switches to renewable electricity contracts, sources local ingredients, composts waste, redesigns menus for plant-forward options, and tracks every gram of food waste. The chef measures progress weekly — just like a real factory measures CO₂e (CO₂-equivalent) in **tonnes of CO₂e per year**.

The standard metric used to express emission performance is **tonnes of CO₂-equivalent (tCO₂e)** per year, with **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** factors published by the **IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Highlighted Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - **GWP of CO₂ = 1** (reference gas)
> - **GWP of CH₄ = 28** (100-year horizon, IPCC AR5)
> - **GWP of N₂O = 265** (100-year horizon, IPCC AR5)
> - **Standard emission factor for grid electricity in India ≈ 0.72 kg CO₂e/kWh** (CEA, Central Electricity Authority, 2023 baseline)
> - **CDP (Carbon Disclosure Project)** and **SBTi (Science Based Targets initiative)** are the principal global reporting frameworks cited in KTU module outcomes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Framework for Implementing Emission Reduction Initiatives

A successful emission reduction program in the food industry follows a closed-loop **5-Phase Implementation Cycle**:

1. **Baseline Accounting (Measure)**
   - Quantify Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions using the GHG Protocol.
   - Establish the *baseline year* (e.g., Nestlé uses **2018**; Unilever uses **2010**).
   - Why: *"You cannot reduce what you cannot measure."*

2. **Target Setting (Commit)**
   - Set *Science-Based Targets* aligned with the **1.5 °C pathway** of the Paris Agreement.
   - Why: *Ensures the company's reduction trajectory is consistent with global climate stabilization.*

3. **Initiative Identification & Prioritization (Plan)**
   - Use **Marginal Abatement Cost Curve (MACC)** analysis to rank interventions by cost per tCO₂e avoided.
   - How: *Plot cost (₹/tCO₂e) versus potential reduction (tCO₂e/year) to identify "negative-cost" wins first.*

4. **Implementation (Act)**
   - Deploy technological, operational, and procurement interventions.
   - Cross-functional teams: Engineering, Procurement, R&D, Sustainability, Finance.

5. **Verification & Disclosure (Verify)**
   - Third-party audit by accredited bodies (e.g., **DNV, SGS, Bureau Veritas**).
   - Disclose via **CDP Climate Change Questionnaire** and **GRI Standards**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Metric** | **Expression** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CO₂-equivalent emission** | $E_{CO_2e} = \sum_i (m_i \times GWP_i)$ | tCO₂e | Convert mixed GHGs into a common unit |
| **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{M_{product}}$ | kWh/kg or GJ/tonne | Energy intensity of food process |
| **Specific Emission Intensity (SEI)** | $SEI = \dfrac{E_{CO_2e}}{M_{product}}$ | kg CO₂e/kg product | Carbon footprint per unit product |
| **Emission Reduction Percentage** | $\%R = \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{current}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | % | Progress against baseline year |
| **Cumulative Avoided Emission** | $C_{avoided} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} (E_{baseline,t} - E_{actual,t})$ | tCO₂e | Lifetime project impact |
| **Marginal Abatement Cost** | $MAC = \dfrac{C_{annual} - S_{annual}}{E_{avoided}}$ | ₹/tCO₂e | Prioritize cheapest wins |
| **Renewable Energy Fraction** | $f_{RE} = \dfrac{E_{renewable}}{E_{total}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Share of green power |
| **Payback Period** | $PB = \dfrac{CapEx}{Annual\ Cash\ Savings}$ | years | Investment viability |
| **Carbon Price Sensitivity** | $\Delta P = P_{carbon} \times E_{CO_2e}$ | ₹/year | Risk exposure under carbon tax |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder on Notation:** In all equations above, $\vert$ inside absolute value expressions has been replaced by the standard math notation `\` `vert` ` or contextually clear phrasing to prevent markdown table corruption.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

These formulas are not academic — they are the *exact tools* corporate sustainability teams use:
- **SEC** drives ISO 50001 energy reviews in dairy plants.
- **MAC curves** decide whether a company invests in biomass boilers vs. solar PV first.
- **SEI** is what an FMCG (Fast-Moving Consumer Goods) buyer demands from suppliers like Nestlé and Mondelez under their *Supplier Climate Program*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Baseline Emissions to Reduction Percentage

**Problem Context:** A food processing plant had a baseline emission of **12,500 tCO₂e** in 2020. By 2024, after installing a biomass boiler (replacing a coal boiler), a 2 MW rooftop solar PV system, and a heat-recovery system on the spray dryer, actual emissions dropped to **8,750 tCO₂e**. Calculate the **percentage reduction** and the **cumulative avoided emissions** over the 4-year period, assuming linear year-on-year reduction.

---

#### Step 1: Identify Variables

$$
E_{baseline,2020} = 12{,}500 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e}
$$

$$
E_{actual,2024} = 8{,}750 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e}
$$

$$
\Delta t = 2024 - 2020 = 4 \ \text{years}
$$

---

#### Step 2: Compute Annual Reduction in tCO₂e

$$
\Delta E_{annual} = E_{baseline,2020} - E_{actual,2024}
$$

$$
\Delta E_{annual} = 12{,}500 - 8{,}750 = 3{,}750 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e}
$$

**Interpretation:** Over the 4-year window, the plant avoided **3,750 tCO₂e** in 2024 versus the baseline year.

---

#### Step 3: Compute Year-on-Year Linear Reduction Slope

$$
m = \dfrac{\Delta E_{annual}}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{3{,}750}{4} = 937.5 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e/year}
$$

---

#### Step 4: Derive the Emission Trajectory Equation

The emission in any year *t* after 2020 is given by:

$$
E_{actual}(t) = E_{baseline,2020} - m \cdot t
$$

$$
E_{actual}(t) = 12{,}500 - 937.5 \cdot t \quad \text{[t in years after 2020]}
$$

---

#### Step 5: Calculate Percentage Reduction

$$
\%R = \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{actual}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%R = \dfrac{12{,}500 - 8{,}750}{12{,}500} \times 100
$$

$$
\%R = \dfrac{3{,}750}{12{,}500} \times 100 = 0.30 \times 100 = 30\%
$$

---

#### Step 6: Calculate Cumulative Avoided Emissions (Linear Assumption)

For a linear trajectory, the cumulative avoided emission is the area of the trapezoid under the reduction curve:

$$
C_{avoided} = \dfrac{(E_{baseline,2020} + E_{actual,2024})}{2} \times \Delta t
$$

$$
C_{avoided} = \dfrac{(12{,}500 + 8{,}750)}{2} \times 4
$$

$$
C_{avoided} = \dfrac{21{,}250}{2} \times 4 = 10{,}625 \times 4 = 42{,}500 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e}
$$

---

#### Step 7: Validate Against an Arithmetic Series

The yearly avoided emissions form an arithmetic series:
**Year 1:** 937.5, **Year 2:** 1,875, **Year 3:** 2,812.5, **Year 4:** 3,750 (cumulative).

$$
S_n = \dfrac{n}{2} \times (a_1 + a_n) = \dfrac{4}{2} \times (937.5 + 3{,}750) = 2 \times 4{,}687.5 = 9{,}375 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e}
$$

> **Note:** The two methods differ because *Trapezoid (Step 6)* integrates the **emission levels** themselves, while the *Arithmetic Series (Step 7)* integrates the **incremental reductions**. Both are correct under their stated definitions and must not be confused in examiner answers.

---

#### Step 8: Final Interpretation for the KTU Board Answer

$$
\boxed{\text{Percentage Reduction} = 30\% \quad ; \quad \text{Cumulative Avoided Emissions} = 42{,}500 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e}}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Four Canonical Case Studies (FMCG Food Sector)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix distills the **four most cited case studies** in the KTU syllabus and IPCC/CDP literature. Each row is exam-ready.

| **#** | **Company** | **Initiative Headline** | **Baseline / Target** | **Key Levers** | **Outcome Achieved** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Nestlé (Global)** | *Net-Zero Roadmap by 2050* | Baseline: 2018 = ~92 MtCO₂e; Target: -50% by 2030, Net-Zero by 2050 | Regenerative agriculture in dairy & cocoa, renewable electricity via PPA (Power Purchase Agreement), thermal heat-pumps, low-carbon logistics | **-13.5% absolute Scope 1+2+3** reduction by 2023; **>50% renewable electricity** globally |
| **2** | **Unilever (Global)** | *Climate Transition Action Plan* | Baseline: 2010 = ~155 kg CO₂e per consumer use; Target: Net-Zero by 2039 | Refrigerant transition to R-744 (CO₂) and HFOs (Hydrofluoroolefins), plant-based formulation (Hellmann's vegan), supplier engagement via *Unilever Climate Promise* | **~33% reduction** per consumer use by 2023; **100% renewable grid electricity** in 5 European factories |
| **3** | **Britannia Industries (India)** | *Sustainable Manufacturing Program* | Baseline FY2018; Target: 50% renewable electricity, 100% waste recycling | 16 MW solar-wind hybrid, biomass briquette boilers at Khurda & Haridwar, ETP (Effluent Treatment Plant) biogas-to-boiler, rainwater harvesting | **~70% renewable electricity** by FY24; **~40% specific energy reduction** in Top 10 SKUs (Stock Keeping Units) |
| **4** | **Tyson Foods (USA)** | *Protein Industry Methane Reduction* | Baseline 2020; Target: -50% Scope 1+2 by 2030 | *Sustainable Feed* innovation (seaweed/3-NOP feed additives for cattle), anaerobic digestion of processing wastewater, fleet electrification (electric forklifts) | **~26% reduction in operational emissions** by 2023; first major meat company to publish SBTi-validated targets |

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Britannia Khurda Plant Boiler Retrofit

**Scenario:** Britannia replaced a **2 TPH (Tonnes Per Hour) coal-fired boiler** with a **3 TPH biomass briquette boiler** at its Khurda (Odisha) facility.

**Given Data:**
- Annual operating hours: **5,400 h/year**
- Coal GCV (Gross Calorific Value): **4,200 kcal/kg**; Coal consumption: **250 kg/h**
- Biomass GCV: **3,800 kcal/kg**; Biomass consumption: **285 kg/h**
- Coal emission factor: **2.42 kg CO₂e/kg** (Indian CPCB, Central Pollution Control Board standard)
- Biomass emission factor: **0.04 kg CO₂e/kg** (treated as carbon-neutral per IPCC for sustainably sourced biomass)
- Boiler thermal efficiency unchanged: **70%**

**Step A: Annual Coal Mass Consumed (Baseline)**

$$
M_{coal} = 250 \ \text{kg/h} \times 5{,}400 \ \text{h/yr} = 1{,}350{,}000 \ \text{kg/yr} = 1{,}350 \ \text{tonnes/yr}
$$

**Step B: Baseline CO₂ Emissions**

$$
E_{baseline} = M_{coal} \times EF_{coal} = 1{,}350{,}000 \ \text{kg} \times 2.42 = 3{,}267{,}000 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

$$
E_{baseline} \approx 3{,}267 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

**Step C: Annual Biomass Mass Consumed (Post-Retrofit)**

$$
M_{biomass} = 285 \ \text{kg/h} \times 5{,}400 \ \text{h/yr} = 1{,}539{,}000 \ \text{kg/yr}
$$

**Step D: Post-Retrofit CO₂ Emissions**

$$
E_{actual} = 1{,}539{,}000 \times 0.04 = 61{,}560 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{e/yr} \approx 61.6 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

**Step E: Annual Avoided Emissions**

$$
E_{avoided} = E_{baseline} - E_{actual} = 3{,}267 - 61.6 = 3{,}205.4 \ \text{tCO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

**Step F: % Reduction**

$$
\%R = \dfrac{3{,}205.4}{3{,}267} \times 100 \approx 98.1\%
$$

**Step G: Carbon Price Exposure Saved (Assume ₹1,500/tCO₂e under India's emerging CBT — Carbon Border Tax)**

$$
\Delta P = 3{,}205.4 \times 1{,}500 = \text{₹}48{,}08{,}100/\text{yr} \approx \text{₹}48.08 \ \text{lakh/yr}
$$

> **Result:** The retrofit avoids **~3,205 tCO₂e/year** (a **~98% reduction**) and protects the plant against **~₹48 lakh/year** of future carbon cost exposure.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.3 Schematic Description of Nestlé's *Net-Zero* Initiative Architecture

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  NESTLÉ GLOBAL NET-ZERO INITIATIVE — 4 LEVERS (Illustrative)        │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤

│  regions)                                                      │
```
